        formula = term(:A) ~ sum(term.(Symbol.(names(df)[1:end-1])))
        model = glm(formula, df, Binomial(), LogitLink())
        π_hat = predict(model, df, type="response")
        
    elseif method == "xgboost"
        # Gradient boosting with XGBoost
        X_table = MLJ.table(X)
        model = XGBoostClassifier(num_round=100, max_depth=6, eta=0.1)
        mach = machine(model, X_table, categorical(A))
        fit!(mach)
        π_hat = pdf.(predict(mach, X_table), 1)
        
    elseif method == "neural_net"
        # Neural network with MLJFlux
        X_table = MLJ.table(X)
        builder = MLJFlux.Short(n_hidden=32, dropout=0.2, σ=relu)
        model = NeuralNetworkClassifier(builder=builder, epochs=100, batch_size=32)
        mach = machine(model, X_table, categorical(A))
        fit!(mach)
        π_hat = pdf.(predict(mach, X_table), 1)
        
    else
        error("Method must be 'logistic', 'xgboost', or 'neural_net'")
    end
    
    return π_hat
end

# Example usage with simulated biomedical data
function simulate_medical_study(n=1000)
    # Simulate patient characteristics
    age = rand(Normal(65, 15), n)
    bmi = rand(Normal(28, 5), n)
    severity_score = rand(Normal(50, 20), n)
    comorbidity_count = rand(Poisson(2), n)
    
    X = hcat(age, bmi, severity_score, comorbidity_count)
    
    # Treatment assignment depends nonlinearly on covariates
    logit_π = -2.0 .+ 0.03 .* age .+ 0.1 .* bmi .+ 0.02 .* severity_score .+ 
              0.3 .* comorbidity_count .+ 0.001 .* age .* bmi
    π_true = 1 ./ (1 .+ exp.(-logit_π))
    A = rand.(Bernoulli.(π_true))
    
    return X, A, π_true
end

# Demonstrate estimation methods
X, A, π_true = simulate_medical_study()
π_logistic = estimate_propensity_score(X, A, method="logistic")
π_xgboost = estimate_propensity_score(X, A, method="xgboost")

println("Correlation with true propensity score:")
println("Logistic: ", cor(π_true, π_logistic))
println("XGBoost: ", cor(π_true, π_xgboost))

4.3 4.3 Propensity Score Matching

Matching on the propensity score creates balanced subsets of treated and control units by pairing units with similar estimated propensity scores. This approach directly implements the intuition that units with similar propensity scores are exchangeable.

4.3.1 4.3.1 Nearest Neighbor Matching

The simplest matching approach pairs each treated unit with the control unit having the closest propensity score.

Definition 4.2 (Nearest Neighbor Matching). For each treated unit i with propensity score π̂ᵢ, the matched control unit j(i) is chosen as: j(i) = arg min_{j: Aⱼ=0} |π̂ᵢ - π̂ⱼ|

With Replacement: Each control unit can be matched to multiple treated units, maximizing the number of matches but potentially reducing precision.

Without Replacement: Each control unit is used at most once, ensuring independent matches but potentially discarding some treated units.

4.3.2 4.3.2 Caliper Matching

Caliper matching imposes a maximum allowable distance between matched units to ensure adequate balance.

Definition 4.3 (Caliper Matching). A treated unit i is matched to control unit j(i) only if |π̂ᵢ - π̂ⱼ(i)| ≤ c, where c is the caliper width.

A common choice is c = 0.2 × SD(π̂), where SD(π̂) is the standard deviation of estimated propensity scores. This specification balances the competing goals of maximizing matches and ensuring adequate balance.

4.3.3 4.3.3 Optimal Matching

Optimal matching formulates the matching problem as a minimum-cost network flow problem, simultaneously choosing all matches to minimize the total distance.

Definition 4.4 (Optimal Matching). Let D(i,j) = |π̂ᵢ - π̂ⱼ| be the distance between units i and j. Optimal matching solves: min ∑ᵢ,ⱼ D(i,j) × Mᵢⱼ subject to ∑ⱼ Mᵢⱼ ≤ 1 for all treated i, ∑ᵢ Mᵢⱼ ≤ 1 for all control j, and Mᵢⱼ ∈ {0,1}

While computationally more demanding than greedy approaches, optimal matching often achieves superior balance.

4.3.4 4.3.4 Implementation and Diagnostics

using Distances, Hungarian, Plots

function propensity_score_matching(π_treated, π_control, Y_treated, Y_control; 
                                  method="nearest", caliper=nothing, with_replacement=false)
    n_treated = length(π_treated)
    n_control = length(π_control)
    
    if method == "nearest"
        matches = Int[]
        used_controls = Set{Int}()
        
        for i in 1:n_treated
            distances = abs.(π_treated[i] .- π_control)
            
            if !with_replacement
                # Exclude already used controls
                distances[collect(used_controls)] .= Inf
            end
            
            best_match = argmin(distances)
            
            # Check caliper constraint
            if caliper !== nothing && distances[best_match] > caliper
                continue  # Skip this treated unit
            end
            
            push!(matches, best_match)
            if !with_replacement
                push!(used_controls, best_match)
            end
        end
        
        # Compute treatment effect estimate
        if length(matches) > 0
            matched_treated_outcomes = Y_treated[1:length(matches)]
            matched_control_outcomes = Y_control[matches]
            ate_estimate = mean(matched_treated_outcomes - matched_control_outcomes)
        else
            ate_estimate = NaN
        end
        
    elseif method == "optimal"
        # Create distance matrix
        dist_matrix = [abs(π_t - π_c) for π_t in π_treated, π_c in π_control]
        
        # Apply caliper constraint
        if caliper !== nothing
            dist_matrix[dist_matrix .> caliper] .= 1e6
        end
        
        # Solve assignment problem
        assignment, cost = hungarian(dist_matrix)
        
        # Extract valid matches
        valid_matches = findall(assignment .≤ n_control)
        matches = assignment[valid_matches]
        
        if length(matches) > 0
            matched_treated_outcomes = Y_treated[valid_matches]
            matched_control_outcomes = Y_control[matches]
            ate_estimate = mean(matched_treated_outcomes - matched_control_outcomes)
        else
            ate_estimate = NaN
        end
    end
    
    return ate_estimate, matches
end

# Example: Matching in clinical trial emulation
function simulate_clinical_outcomes(X, A, π_true)
    n = size(X, 1)
    # Outcome depends on covariates and treatment
    Y0 = 50 .+ 0.5 .* X[:, 1] .+ 0.2 .* X[:, 2] .+ rand(Normal(0, 10), n)  # Control outcome
    treatment_effect = 5 .+ 0.1 .* X[:, 1]  # Heterogeneous treatment effect
    Y1 = Y0 .+ treatment_effect
    
    Y = A .* Y1 .+ (1 .- A) .* Y0
    return Y, Y0, Y1
end

# Generate data and estimate propensity scores
X, A, π_true = simulate_medical_study(500)
Y, Y0, Y1 = simulate_clinical_outcomes(X, A, π_true)
π_hat = estimate_propensity_score(X, A, method="logistic")

# Separate treated and control units
treated_idx = findall(A .== 1)
control_idx = findall(A .== 0)

# Perform matching
ate_nearest, matches_nearest = propensity_score_matching(
    π_hat[treated_idx], π_hat[control_idx], Y[treated_idx], Y[control_idx],
    method="nearest", with_replacement=false
)

ate_caliper, matches_caliper = propensity_score_matching(
    π_hat[treated_idx], π_hat[control_idx], Y[treated_idx], Y[control_idx],
    method="nearest", caliper=0.1, with_replacement=false
)

println("True ATE: ", mean(Y1 - Y0))
println("Nearest neighbor matching ATE: ", ate_nearest)
println("Caliper matching ATE: ", ate_caliper)

4.4 4.4 Propensity Score Weighting

Propensity score weighting provides an alternative to matching that uses all available data by reweighting observations to achieve balance. This approach avoids discarding unmatched units and can improve efficiency.

4.4.1 4.4.1 Inverse Probability Weighting (IPW)

The inverse probability weighting estimator reweights observations by the inverse of their probability of receiving their observed treatment.

Definition 4.5 (IPW Weights). For unit i with treatment Aᵢ and propensity score π̂ᵢ, the IPW weight is: wᵢ = Aᵢ/π̂ᵢ + (1 - Aᵢ)/(1 - π̂ᵢ)

Theorem 4.3 (IPW Estimator). Under unconfoundedness and positivity, the IPW estimator is unbiased for the average treatment effect: τ̂ᴵᴾᵂ = (1/n) ∑ᵢ wᵢYᵢ = (1/n) ∑ᵢ [AᵢYᵢ/π̂ᵢ - (1-Aᵢ)Yᵢ/(1-π̂ᵢ)]

Proof. The expectation of the IPW estimator is: E[τ̂ᴵᴾᵂ] = E[AY/π(X) - (1-A)Y/(1-π(X))] = E[E[AY/π(X) - (1-A)Y/(1-π(X)) | X]] = E[π(X)E[Y(1)|X]/π(X) - (1-π(X))E[Y(0)|X]/(1-π(X))] = E[Y(1) - Y(0)] = τ

where we use the unconfoundedness assumption E[Y(1)|A,X] = E[Y(1)|X]. □

4.4.2 4.4.2 Stabilized Weights

IPW weights can exhibit high variance when propensity scores approach 0 or 1. Stabilized weights address this issue by incorporating marginal treatment probabilities.

Definition 4.6 (Stabilized Weights). The stabilized weight for unit i is: wᵢˢᵗᵃᵇ = [Aᵢ · P(A=1) + (1-Aᵢ) · P(A=0)] / [Aᵢ · π̂ᵢ + (1-Aᵢ) · (1-π̂ᵢ)]

Stabilized weights have mean 1 and typically exhibit lower variance than standard IPW weights, leading to more stable estimation.

4.4.3 4.4.3 Weight Trimming

Extreme propensity score values can produce unstable weights. Trimming excludes observations with propensity scores outside a specified range [α, 1-α], where α is often chosen as 0.05 or 0.1.

function ipw_estimation(Y, A, π_hat; stabilized=false, trim_level=0.05)
    n = length(Y)
    
    # Trim extreme propensity scores
    if trim_level > 0
        keep_idx = findall(trim_level .<= π_hat .<= (1 - trim_level))
        Y, A, π_hat = Y[keep_idx], A[keep_idx], π_hat[keep_idx]
        n = length(Y)
    end
    
    if stabilized
        # Stabilized weights
        p_treat = mean(A)
        weights = (A .* p_treat ./ π_hat) .+ ((1 .- A) .* (1 - p_treat) ./ (1 .- π_hat))
    else
        # Standard IPW weights
        weights = A ./ π_hat .+ (1 .- A) ./ (1 .- π_hat)
    end
    
    # IPW estimator
    ate_ipw = sum(weights .* Y) / sum(weights) - sum(weights .* (1 .- A) .* Y) / sum(weights .* (1 .- A))
    
    # Alternative formulation
    ate_ipw_alt = mean((A ./ π_hat .- (1 .- A) ./ (1 .- π_hat)) .* Y)
    
    # Variance estimation (Lunceford & Davidian, 2004)
    μ1_hat = sum(A .* Y ./ π_hat) / sum(A ./ π_hat)
    μ0_hat = sum((1 .- A) .* Y ./ (1 .- π_hat)) / sum((1 .- A) ./ (1 .- π_hat))
    
    var_est = var((A .* (Y .- μ1_hat) ./ π_hat) .- ((1 .- A) .* (Y .- μ0_hat) ./ (1 .- π_hat))) / n
    se_est = sqrt(var_est)
    
    return ate_ipw_alt, se_est, weights
end

# Demonstrate IPW with different specifications
ate_ipw, se_ipw, weights = ipw_estimation(Y, A, π_hat, stabilized=false)
ate_ipw_stab, se_ipw_stab, weights_stab = ipw_estimation(Y, A, π_hat, stabilized=true)
ate_ipw_trim, se_ipw_trim, weights_trim = ipw_estimation(Y, A, π_hat, stabilized=true, trim_level=0.1)

println("IPW Results:")
println("Standard IPW: $(round(ate_ipw, digits=3)) (SE: $(round(se_ipw, digits=3)))")
println("Stabilized IPW: $(round(ate_ipw_stab, digits=3)) (SE: $(round(se_ipw_stab, digits=3)))")
println("Trimmed IPW: $(round(ate_ipw_trim, digits=3)) (SE: $(round(se_ipw_trim, digits=3)))")

# Weight diagnostics
println("\nWeight Statistics:")
println("Standard weights - Mean: $(round(mean(weights), digits=2)), Max: $(round(maximum(weights), digits=2))")
println("Stabilized weights - Mean: $(round(mean(weights_stab), digits=2)), Max: $(round(maximum(weights_stab), digits=2))")

4.5 4.5 Propensity Score Stratification

Stratification divides the sample into subgroups (strata) based on propensity score values and estimates treatment effects within each stratum. This approach provides a middle ground between matching and weighting.

4.5.1 4.5.1 Stratification Procedure

Definition 4.7 (Propensity Score Stratification). Divide the propensity score range into K strata such that S₁, S₂, …, Sₖ partition [0,1]. Within stratum k, estimate the treatment effect as: τ̂ₖ = Ȳₖ¹ - Ȳₖ⁰ where Ȳₖᵃ is the mean outcome among units with treatment A = a in stratum k.

The overall average treatment effect is estimated as a weighted average of stratum-specific effects:

τ̂ˢᵗʳᵃᵗ = ∑ₖ wₖ τ̂ₖ

where wₖ represents the weight assigned to stratum k, commonly chosen as the proportion of the total sample in stratum k.

4.5.2 4.5.2 Choosing the Number of Strata

Rosenbaum and Rubin (1984) showed that five strata remove approximately 90% of the bias due to a single confounder. However, optimal stratification depends on the specific application and available sample size.

function propensity_score_stratification(Y, A, π_hat; n_strata=5, weights="proportional")
    n = length(Y)
    
    # Create strata based on propensity score quantiles
    quantiles = quantile(π_hat, range(0, 1, length=n_strata+1))
    quantiles[1] = 0.0  # Ensure inclusion of boundary cases
    quantiles[end] = 1.0
    
    strata_effects = Float64[]
    strata_weights = Float64[]
    strata_sizes = Int[]
    
    for k in 1:n_strata
        # Define stratum
        if k == n_strata
            stratum_idx = findall(quantiles[k] .<= π_hat .<= quantiles[k+1])
        else
            stratum_idx = findall(quantiles[k] .<= π_hat .< quantiles[k+1])
        end
        
        if length(stratum_idx) == 0
            continue
        end
        
        # Extract stratum data
        Y_stratum = Y[stratum_idx]
        A_stratum = A[stratum_idx]
        
        # Check if both treatment groups are present
        if sum(A_stratum) == 0 || sum(A_stratum) == length(A_stratum)
            continue  # Skip strata with no variation in treatment
        end
        
        # Estimate stratum-specific effect
        Y_treated = Y_stratum[A_stratum .== 1]
        Y_control = Y_stratum[A_stratum .== 0]
        
        τ_stratum = mean(Y_treated) - mean(Y_control)
        push!(strata_effects, τ_stratum)
        push!(strata_sizes, length(stratum_idx))
        
        # Determine stratum weight
        if weights == "proportional"
            push!(strata_weights, length(stratum_idx) / n)
        elseif weights == "equal"
            push!(strata_weights, 1 / n_strata)
        end
    end
    
    # Normalize weights
    strata_weights = strata_weights ./ sum(strata_weights)
    
    # Overall effect estimate
    ate_strat = sum(strata_effects .* strata_weights)
    
    return ate_strat, strata_effects, strata_weights, strata_sizes
end

# Apply stratification
ate_strat, effects, weights_strat, sizes = propensity_score_stratification(Y, A, π_hat, n_strata=5)

println("Stratification Results:")
println("Overall ATE: $(round(ate_strat, digits=3))")
println("Stratum-specific effects: ", round.(effects, digits=3))
println("Stratum weights: ", round.(weights_strat, digits=3))
println("Stratum sizes: ", sizes)

4.6 4.6 Doubly Robust Estimation

Doubly robust methods combine propensity score modeling with outcome regression to provide protection against model misspecification. These approaches remain consistent if either the propensity score model or the outcome model is correctly specified.

4.6.1 4.6.1 Augmented Inverse Probability Weighting (AIPW)

The AIPW estimator incorporates both propensity scores and outcome regression models.

Definition 4.8 (AIPW Estimator). Let μ̂₁(x) and μ̂₀(x) be estimated regression functions for the outcomes under treatment and control. The AIPW estimator is: τ̂ᴬᴵᴾᵂ = (1/n) ∑ᵢ [μ̂₁(Xᵢ) - μ̂₀(Xᵢ) + Aᵢ(Yᵢ - μ̂₁(Xᵢ))/π̂ᵢ - (1-Aᵢ)(Yᵢ - μ̂₀(Xᵢ))/(1-π̂ᵢ)]

Theorem 4.4 (Double Robustness). The AIPW estimator is consistent if either: 1. The propensity score model π(x) is correctly specified, or 2. The outcome regression models μ₁(x) and μ₀(x) are correctly specified.

Proof Sketch. When the propensity score is correct, the IPW component provides consistency. When the outcome models are correct, the regression imputation provides consistency. The cross-terms involving misspecification errors cancel out when one model is correct. □

4.6.2 4.6.2 Implementation and Cross-Fitting

Modern implementations often employ cross-fitting (sample splitting) to avoid overfitting bias when using flexible machine learning methods.

using Random

function aipw_estimator(Y, A, X; propensity_method="logistic", outcome_method="linear", 
                       n_folds=2, seed=123)
    Random.seed!(seed)
    n = length(Y)
    fold_size = div(n, n_folds)
    indices = randperm(n)
    
    τ_estimates = Float64[]
    
    for fold in 1:n_folds
        # Define training and test sets
        if fold == n_folds
            test_idx = indices[(fold-1)*fold_size + 1:end]
        else
            test_idx = indices[(fold-1)*fold_size + 1:fold*fold_size]
        end
        train_idx = setdiff(1:n, test_idx)
        
        # Training data
        X_train, A_train, Y_train = X[train_idx, :], A[train_idx], Y[train_idx]
        X_test, A_test, Y_test = X[test_idx, :], A[test_idx], Y[test_idx]
        
        # Fit propensity score model
        π_test = estimate_propensity_score(X_train, A_train, method=propensity_method)[test_idx]
        
        # Fit outcome regression models
        if outcome_method == "linear"
            # Separate models for treated and control
            treated_train_idx = findall(A_train .== 1)
            control_train_idx = findall(A_train .== 0)
            
            if length(treated_train_idx) > 0 && length(control_train_idx) > 0
                # Fit models
                df_treated = DataFrame(X_train[treated_train_idx, :], :auto)
                df_treated.Y = Y_train[treated_train_idx]
                df_control = DataFrame(X_train[control_train_idx, :], :auto)
                df_control.Y = Y_train[control_train_idx]
                
                # Create formulas
                feature_names = Symbol.(names(df_treated)[1:end-1])
                formula = term(:Y) ~ sum(term.(feature_names))
                
                model_1 = lm(formula, df_treated)
                model_0 = lm(formula, df_control)
                
                # Predict on test set
                df_test = DataFrame(X_test, :auto)
                μ1_test = predict(model_1, df_test)
                μ0_test = predict(model_0, df_test)
            else
                # Fallback to overall mean if insufficient data
                μ1_test = fill(mean(Y_train[A_train .== 1]), length(test_idx))
                μ0_test = fill(mean(Y_train[A_train .== 0]), length(test_idx))
            end
        end
        
        # AIPW estimator for this fold
        aipw_components = (μ1_test .- μ0_test) .+ 
                         (A_test .* (Y_test .- μ1_test) ./ π_test) .-
                         ((1 .- A_test) .* (Y_test .- μ0_test) ./ (1 .- π_test))
        
        push!(τ_estimates, mean(aipw_components))
    end
    
    return mean(τ_estimates)
end

# Compare different estimators
println("Method Comparison:")
println("True ATE: $(round(mean(Y1 - Y0), digits=3))")
println("AIPW (logistic + linear): $(round(aipw_estimator(Y, A, X, propensity_method="logistic", outcome_method="linear"), digits=3))")

# Simulate misspecified models
function simulate_complex_data(n=1000)
    X = rand(Normal(0, 1), n, 4)
    # Nonlinear propensity score
    π_true = 1 ./ (1 .+ exp.(-X[:, 1] .- 0.5 .* X[:, 2].^2 .- X[:, 3] .* X[:, 4]))
    A = rand.(Bernoulli.(π_true))


\newpage

# Chapter 5: Instrumental Variables

Instrumental variables (IV) methods provide a powerful framework for causal inference when treatment assignment is not randomized and unmeasured confounders may bias standard regression estimates. The IV approach exploits variation in treatment that is "as good as random" — induced by an instrument that affects the outcome only through its effect on the treatment variable.

This chapter develops the theoretical foundations of IV estimation, examines the key identifying assumptions, and explores both classical econometric applications and modern applications in biostatistics, particularly Mendelian randomization. We begin with the potential outcomes framework for IV, establish the connection to two-stage least squares (2SLS), and address practical challenges including weak instruments and the interpretation of local average treatment effects.

## The IV Framework in Potential Outcomes

Consider the standard causal inference setup with treatment $T_i \in \{0,1\}$, outcome $Y_i$, and observed covariates $\mathbf{X}_i$. The fundamental challenge is that treatment assignment may depend on unmeasured confounders $U_i$ that also influence the outcome, making naive regression estimates of treatment effects causally uninterpretable.

An instrumental variable $Z_i$ provides a solution by introducing exogenous variation in treatment assignment. Under certain assumptions, this variation identifies causal effects even in the presence of unmeasured confounding.

> **Definition 5.1 (Instrumental Variable).** A random variable $Z$ is an instrumental variable for the causal effect of treatment $T$ on outcome $Y$ if it satisfies three conditions:
> 1. **Relevance**: $Z$ is associated with $T$
> 2. **Exclusion Restriction**: $Z$ affects $Y$ only through its effect on $T$
> 3. **Independence**: $Z$ is independent of unmeasured confounders

To formalize these conditions using potential outcomes, let $Y_i(t)$ denote the potential outcome for individual $i$ under treatment level $t$, and let $T_i(z)$ denote the potential treatment level for individual $i$ when the instrument takes value $z$. The observed outcome is $Y_i = Y_i(T_i)$ and observed treatment is $T_i = T_i(Z_i)$.

> **Assumption 5.1 (IV Assumptions).** 
> 1. **Relevance**: $E[T_i(1)] \neq E[T_i(0)]$
> 2. **Exclusion Restriction**: $Y_i(t, z) = Y_i(t)$ for all $t, z$
> 3. **Independence**: $(Y_i(0), Y_i(1), T_i(0), T_i(1)) \perp Z_i$

The exclusion restriction assumption states that the instrument $Z$ does not directly affect the potential outcomes; its only causal pathway to $Y$ operates through treatment $T$. This is often the most contentious assumption in practice.

## The LATE Theorem and Complier Types

A key insight of Angrist, Imbens, and Rubin (1996) is that IV methods typically do not identify average treatment effects for the entire population, but rather local average treatment effects (LATE) for a specific subpopulation.

When treatment and instrument are both binary, we can classify individuals into four types based on their potential treatment responses:

> **Definition 5.2 (Complier Types).** For binary instrument $Z \in \{0,1\}$ and treatment $T \in \{0,1\}$:
> - **Compliers**: $T_i(1) = 1, T_i(0) = 0$ (treatment follows instrument)
> - **Always-takers**: $T_i(1) = 1, T_i(0) = 1$ (always treated)
> - **Never-takers**: $T_i(1) = 0, T_i(0) = 0$ (never treated)
> - **Defiers**: $T_i(1) = 0, T_i(0) = 1$ (treatment opposes instrument)

The monotonicity assumption rules out defiers:

> **Assumption 5.2 (Monotonicity).** $T_i(1) \geq T_i(0)$ for all $i$.

Under the IV assumptions plus monotonicity, the IV estimand identifies the complier average causal effect (CACE):

> **Theorem 5.1 (LATE Theorem).** Under Assumptions 5.1 and 5.2, the IV estimand
> $$\tau_{IV} = \frac{E[Y_i | Z_i = 1] - E[Y_i | Z_i = 0]}{E[T_i | Z_i = 1] - E[T_i | Z_i = 0]}$$
> equals the complier average causal effect:
> $$\tau_{CACE} = E[Y_i(1) - Y_i(0) | T_i(1) > T_i(0)]$$

*Proof.* By the law of total expectation and the definition of complier types:
$$E[Y_i | Z_i = z] = \sum_{type} E[Y_i | Z_i = z, type] \cdot P(type)$$

Under independence, $E[Y_i | Z_i = z, type] = E[Y_i(T_i(z)) | type]$. For compliers with $z=1$: $E[Y_i(T_i(1)) | C] = E[Y_i(1) | C]$. For compliers with $z=0$: $E[Y_i(T_i(0)) | C] = E[Y_i(0) | C]$.

Always-takers have $T_i(1) = T_i(0) = 1$, so their contribution to the numerator is:
$$E[Y_i(1) | A] - E[Y_i(1) | A] = 0$$

Never-takers have $T_i(1) = T_i(0) = 0$, so their contribution is:
$$E[Y_i(0) | N] - E[Y_i(0) | N] = 0$$

By monotonicity, there are no defiers. Therefore:
$$E[Y_i | Z_i = 1] - E[Y_i | Z_i = 0] = P(C) \cdot [E[Y_i(1) | C] - E[Y_i(0) | C]]$$

Similarly:
$$E[T_i | Z_i = 1] - E[T_i | Z_i = 0] = P(C)$$

The result follows immediately. □

This theorem reveals that IV estimation identifies treatment effects specifically for individuals whose treatment status is influenced by the instrument — the compliers. This local nature of IV estimates has important implications for external validity and policy relevance.

## Two-Stage Least Squares

The most common implementation of IV estimation is two-stage least squares (2SLS), which provides a simple computational framework for linear models.

Consider the structural equation:
$$Y_i = \alpha + \beta T_i + \gamma' \mathbf{X}_i + \epsilon_i$$

where $\beta$ is the parameter of interest representing the causal effect of treatment. If treatment is endogenous ($E[T_i \epsilon_i] \neq 0$), OLS estimation of $\beta$ will be biased.

The 2SLS procedure consists of two regression stages:

> **Algorithm 5.1 (Two-Stage Least Squares).**
> 
> **Stage 1:** Regress treatment on instruments and controls:
> $$T_i = \pi_0 + \pi_1 Z_i + \pi_2' \mathbf{X}_i + v_i$$
> Obtain fitted values $\hat{T}_i = \hat{\pi}_0 + \hat{\pi}_1 Z_i + \hat{\pi}_2' \mathbf{X}_i$.
> 
> **Stage 2:** Regress outcome on fitted treatment and controls:
> $$Y_i = \alpha + \beta \hat{T}_i + \gamma' \mathbf{X}_i + u_i$$
> The coefficient $\hat{\beta}_{2SLS}$ is the IV estimate.

The 2SLS estimator can be expressed in closed form. For the exactly identified case with one instrument:

$$\hat{\beta}_{2SLS} = \frac{(\mathbf{Z} - \bar{Z}\mathbf{1})'(\mathbf{Y} - \bar{Y}\mathbf{1})}{(\mathbf{Z} - \bar{Z}\mathbf{1})'(\mathbf{T} - \bar{T}\mathbf{1})}$$

where $\mathbf{Z}$, $\mathbf{Y}$, and $\mathbf{T}$ are column vectors of instrument, outcome, and treatment values.

> **Theorem 5.2 (Consistency of 2SLS).** Under the IV assumptions and standard regularity conditions, the 2SLS estimator is consistent:
> $$\text{plim } \hat{\beta}_{2SLS} = \beta$$

*Proof.* By the continuous mapping theorem and the law of large numbers:
$$\text{plim } \hat{\beta}_{2SLS} = \frac{\text{Cov}(Z_i, Y_i)}{\text{Cov}(Z_i, T_i)} = \frac{E[Z_i Y_i] - E[Z_i]E[Y_i]}{E[Z_i T_i] - E[Z_i]E[T_i]}$$

Under the IV assumptions, this equals the causal parameter $\beta$. □

Let's implement 2SLS estimation in Julia:

```julia
using LinearAlgebra, Statistics, Distributions, DataFrames, GLM

"""
Two-Stage Least Squares estimation
"""
function tsls(Y::Vector, T::Vector, Z::Vector, X::Matrix=ones(length(Y), 1))
    n = length(Y)
    
    # Stage 1: Regress T on Z and X
    stage1_X = hcat(Z, X)
    π̂ = stage1_X \ T
    T_hat = stage1_X * π̂
    
    # Stage 2: Regress Y on T_hat and X
    stage2_X = hcat(T_hat, X)
    coeffs = stage2_X \ Y
    
    β̂ = coeffs[1]  # IV estimate
    
    # Calculate standard errors (heteroskedasticity-robust)
    residuals = Y - stage2_X * coeffs
    
    # Correct for generated regressor in stage 2
    # Use Z instead of T_hat for variance calculation
    iv_X = hcat(Z, X)
    P_z = iv_X * inv(iv_X' * iv_X) * iv_X'
    
    # Robust variance estimator
    Ω = Diagonal(residuals.^2)
    V = inv(stage2_X' * P_z * stage2_X) * (stage2_X' * P_z * Ω * P_z * stage2_X) * inv(stage2_X' * P_z * stage2_X)
    
    se_β = sqrt(V[1,1])
    
    return (estimate=β̂, se=se_β, first_stage_coeff=π̂[1])
end

# Example: Simulated data with confounding
function simulate_iv_data(n=1000; β=2.0, γ=1.5, α=0.5)
    # Unmeasured confounder
    U = randn(n)
    
    # Instrument (independent of U)
    Z = rand(Bernoulli(0.5), n)
    
    # Treatment depends on instrument and confounder
    T_latent = 0.5 * Z + 0.3 * U + randn(n) * 0.1
    T = Float64.(T_latent .> 0)
    
    # Outcome depends on treatment and confounder
    Y = α + β * T + γ * U + randn(n) * 0.5
    
    return (Y=Y, T=T, Z=Z, U=U)
end

# Generate data and compare OLS vs IV
data = simulate_iv_data(1000)

# OLS (biased due to confounding)
ols_result = lm(@formula(Y ~ T), DataFrame(Y=data.Y, T=data.T))
β̂_ols = coef(ols_result)[2]

# IV estimation
iv_result = tsls(data.Y, data.T, Float64.(data.Z))

println("True causal effect: β = 2.0")
println("OLS estimate: $(round(β̂_ols, digits=3))")
println("IV estimate: $(round(iv_result.estimate, digits=3)) (SE: $(round(iv_result.se, digits=3)))")
println("First stage F-stat: $(round(iv_result.first_stage_coeff^2 * 250, digits=2))")

This implementation demonstrates the key components of 2SLS: the first stage prediction of endogenous treatment using instruments, and the second stage regression using fitted treatment values. The standard error calculation accounts for the generated regressor problem inherent in two-stage procedures.

4.7 Weak Instruments and Finite Sample Bias

A critical challenge in IV estimation is the weak instruments problem. When instruments are only weakly correlated with the endogenous treatment, IV estimators can exhibit severe finite-sample bias even when they remain consistent.

Definition 5.3 (Weak Instruments). Instruments are considered weak when the first-stage F-statistic for joint significance of instruments is small, typically below 10.

The finite-sample bias of 2SLS can be approximated as:

Theorem 5.3 (2SLS Finite Sample Bias). When instruments are weak, the finite-sample bias of the 2SLS estimator is approximately: \[E[\hat{\beta}_{2SLS} - \beta] \approx \frac{\text{Cov}(Z_i, \epsilon_i)}{\text{Cov}(Z_i, T_i)} \cdot \frac{1}{F + 1}\] where \(F\) is the first-stage F-statistic.

This shows that bias increases as instruments become weaker (smaller \(F\)) and is proportional to any violation of the exclusion restriction.

Let’s examine weak instrument bias computationally:

"""
Simulate weak instrument bias
"""
function weak_instrument_simulation(n=500, n_sims=1000; instrument_strength=0.1)
    iv_estimates = Float64[]
    ols_estimates = Float64[]
    first_stage_f_stats = Float64[]
    
    for sim in 1:n_sims
        # Generate data with weak instrument
        U = randn(n)  # Confounder
        Z = randn(n)  # Instrument
        
        # Weak first stage
        T = instrument_strength * Z + 0.5 * U + randn(n)
        
        # Outcome
        Y = 1.0 * T + 0.8 * U + randn(n)
        
        # OLS
        β̂_ols = cov(T, Y) / var(T)
        push!(ols_estimates, β̂_ols)
        
        # IV
        β̂_iv = cov(Z, Y) / cov(Z, T)
        push!(iv_estimates, β̂_iv)
        
        # First stage F-stat (approximate)
        f_stat = (cov(Z, T)^2 / var(Z)) / (var(T - cov(Z,T)/var(Z) * Z) / (n-2))
        push!(first_stage_f_stats, f_stat)
    end
    
    return (
        iv_bias = mean(iv_estimates) - 1.0,
        ols_bias = mean(ols_estimates) - 1.0,
        mean_f_stat = mean(first_stage_f_stats),
        iv_rmse = sqrt(mean((iv_estimates .- 1.0).^2))
    )
end

# Compare strong vs weak instruments
println("Strong Instruments (strength = 0.5):")
strong_results = weak_instrument_simulation(500, 1000, instrument_strength=0.5)
println("IV bias: $(round(strong_results.iv_bias, digits=3))")
println("F-stat: $(round(strong_results.mean_f_stat, digits=1))")
println("IV RMSE: $(round(strong_results.iv_rmse, digits=3))")

println("\nWeak Instruments (strength = 0.1):")
weak_results = weak_instrument_simulation(500, 1000, instrument_strength=0.1)
println("IV bias: $(round(weak_results.iv_bias, digits=3))")
println("F-stat: $(round(weak_results.mean_f_stat, digits=1))")
println("IV RMSE: $(round(weak_results.iv_rmse, digits=3))")

4.8 The Anderson-Rubin Test

When instruments are weak, standard inference procedures based on asymptotic normality of the 2SLS estimator can be misleading. The Anderson-Rubin (AR) test provides a robust alternative that maintains correct size regardless of instrument strength.

Definition 5.4 (Anderson-Rubin Test). The AR test for \(H_0: \beta = \beta_0\) is based on the reduced-form regression: \[Y_i - \beta_0 T_i = \delta_0 + \delta_1 Z_i + \delta_2' \mathbf{X}_i + \eta_i\] Under the null and IV assumptions, \(\delta_1 = 0\).

The AR test statistic is the F-statistic for \(\delta_1 = 0\) in this reduced-form regression. Importantly, this test is robust to weak instruments because it does not rely on the asymptotic distribution of the 2SLS estimator.

"""
Anderson-Rubin test for IV coefficient
"""
function anderson_rubin_test(Y::Vector, T::Vector, Z::Vector, β₀::Float64; 
                           X::Matrix=ones(length(Y), 1))
    n = length(Y)
    
    # Reduced form: Y - β₀T on Z and X
    Y_transformed = Y - β₀ * T
    Z_X = hcat(Z, X)
    
    # Unrestricted regression
    unrestricted_coeff = Z_X \ Y_transformed
    unrestricted_resid = Y_transformed - Z_X * unrestricted_coeff
    ssr_unrestricted = sum(unrestricted_resid.^2)
    
    # Restricted regression (δ₁ = 0)
    restricted_coeff = X \ Y_transformed
    restricted_resid = Y_transformed - X * restricted_coeff
    ssr_restricted = sum(restricted_resid.^2)
    
    # F-statistic
    num_restrictions = 1
    df_unrestricted = n - size(Z_X, 2)
    
    f_stat = ((ssr_restricted - ssr_unrestricted) / num_restrictions) / 
             (ssr_unrestricted / df_unrestricted)
    
    # P-value
    p_value = 1 - cdf(FDist(num_restrictions, df_unrestricted), f_stat)
    
    return (f_stat=f_stat, p_value=p_value, df=(num_restrictions, df_unrestricted))
end

# Example: Test different null hypotheses
data = simulate_iv_data(500)

# Test true value β = 2.0
ar_true = anderson_rubin_test(data.Y, data.T, Float64.(data.Z), 2.0)
println("AR test for β = 2.0: F = $(round(ar_true.f_stat, digits=2)), p = $(round(ar_true.p_value, digits=3))")

# Test wrong value β = 0.0
ar_wrong = anderson_rubin_test(data.Y, data.T, Float64.(data.Z), 0.0)
println("AR test for β = 0.0: F = $(round(ar_wrong.f_stat, digits=2)), p = $(round(ar_wrong.p_value, digits=3))")

4.9 Mendelian Randomization

Mendelian randomization (MR) represents one of the most important modern applications of instrumental variables in biostatistics and epidemiology. It exploits the random assortment of genetic variants during meiosis to create natural experiments for causal inference.

The key insight is that genetic variants can serve as instruments for modifiable exposures, enabling causal inference about the effects of these exposures on health outcomes. This approach is particularly valuable for studying exposures that are difficult to randomize experimentally, such as dietary patterns, alcohol consumption, or biomarker levels.

Definition 5.5 (Mendelian Randomization). MR uses genetic variants as instrumental variables to estimate the causal effect of an exposure on an outcome, relying on three core assumptions: 1. Genetic association: The genetic variant is associated with the exposure 2. Independence: The genetic variant is independent of confounders 3. Exclusion restriction: The genetic variant affects the outcome only through the exposure

The independence assumption is supported by Mendel’s laws of inheritance — genetic variants are randomly allocated at conception, creating a natural randomization process. However, the exclusion restriction can be violated by pleiotropy, where genetic variants have direct effects on the outcome through biological pathways other than the exposure.

4.9.1 Single-Sample MR

In single-sample MR, genetic, exposure, and outcome data are available for the same individuals. This allows standard IV estimation using individual-level data.

using Random, Distributions, LinearAlgebra

"""
Simulate Mendelian randomization data
"""
function simulate_mr_data(n=10000; β_true=0.5, pleiotropy=0.0)
    # Genetic variant (SNP) - typically coded as 0, 1, 2 copies
    maf = 0.3  # Minor allele frequency
    G = rand(Binomial(2, maf), n)
    
    # Confounders (lifestyle, environment)
    U = randn(n)
    
    # Exposure (e.g., LDL cholesterol)
    # Genetic effect + confounders + noise
    X = 0.4 * G + 0.6 * U + randn(n) * 0.5
    
    # Outcome (e.g., cardiovascular disease risk)
    # Causal effect of exposure + genetic pleiotropy + confounders + noise
    Y = β_true * X + pleiotropy * G + 0.8 * U + randn(n) * 0.7
    
    return (G=G, X=X, Y=Y, U=U)
end

"""
Mendelian randomization analysis
"""
function mendelian_randomization(G::Vector, X::Vector, Y::Vector)
    # Check instrument strength
    first_stage = lm(@formula(X ~ G), DataFrame(X=X, G=G))
    f_stat = ftest(first_stage).fstat[1]
    
    if f_stat < 10
        @warn "Weak instrument detected (F = $(round(f_stat, digits=2)))"
    end
    
    # IV estimation
    β̂_iv = cov(G, Y) / cov(G, X)
    
    # Standard error using delta method
    n = length(G)
    γ̂_gx = cov(G, X) / var(G)  # First stage coefficient
    γ̂_gy = cov(G, Y) / var(G)  # Reduced form coefficient
    
    # Residual variance in second stage
    ε̂ = Y - β̂_iv * X
    σ̂²_ε = var(ε̂)
    
    # Delta method variance
    se_iv = sqrt(σ̂²_ε / (n * var(G) * γ̂_gx^2))
    
    # 95% confidence interval
    ci_lower = β̂_iv - 1.96 * se_iv
    ci_upper = β̂_iv + 1.96 * se_iv
    
    return (
        estimate = β̂_iv,
        se = se_iv,
        ci = (ci_lower, ci_upper),
        f_stat = f_stat,
        first_stage_coeff = γ̂_gx
    )
end

# Simulate MR study without pleiotropy
Random.seed!(123)
data_clean = simulate_mr_data(5000, β_true=0.5, pleiotropy=0.0)

mr_result_clean = mendelian_randomization(data_clean.G, data_clean.X, data_clean.Y)
println("Clean MR (no pleiotropy):")
println("Estimate: $(round(mr_result_clean.estimate, digits=3)) ($(round(mr_result_clean.ci[1], digits=3)), $(round(mr_result_clean.ci[2], digits=3)))")
println("F-statistic: $(round(mr_result_clean.f_stat, digits=1))")

# Simulate with pleiotropy
data_pleiotropy = simulate_mr_data(5000, β_true=0.5, pleiotropy=0.3)
mr_result_pleiotropy = mendelian_randomization(data_pleiotropy.G, data_pleiotropy.X, data_pleiotropy.Y)
println("\nMR with pleiotropy:")
println("Estimate: $(round(mr_result_pleiotropy.estimate, digits=3)) ($(round(mr_result_pleiotropy.ci[1], digits=3)), $(round(mr_result_pleiotropy.ci[2], digits=3)))")

4.9.2 Two-Sample MR

Two-sample MR has become increasingly popular with the availability of large genome-wide association study (GWAS) summary statistics. In this approach, genetic associations with the exposure and outcome are estimated in separate samples, then combined to obtain causal estimates.

Let \(\hat{\Gamma}_{GX}\) denote the estimated genetic association with exposure (from exposure GWAS) and \(\hat{\Gamma}_{GY}\) denote the estimated genetic association with outcome (from outcome GWAS). The two-sample MR estimate is:

\[\hat{\beta}_{MR} = \frac{\hat{\Gamma}_{GY}}{\hat{\Gamma}_{GX}}\]

"""
Two-sample Mendelian randomization
"""
function two_sample_mr(Γ̂_GX::Vector, Γ̂_GY::Vector, se_GX::Vector, se_GY::Vector)
    # Inverse variance weighted (IVW) meta-analysis
    # Weights proportional to precision of exposure associations
    weights = 1 ./ se_GX.^2
    
    # IVW estimate
    β̂_ivw = sum(weights .* (Γ̂_GY ./ Γ̂_GX)) / sum(weights)
    
    # Standard error
    se_ivw = sqrt(1 / sum(weights))
    
    # Individual SNP ratio estimates for heterogeneity testing
    ratio_estimates = Γ̂_GY ./ Γ̂_GX
    
    # Cochran's Q statistic for heterogeneity
    Q = sum(weights .* (ratio_estimates .- β̂_ivw).^2)
    df = length(Γ̂_GX) - 1
    heterogeneity_p = 1 - cdf(Chisq(df), Q)
    
    return (
        estimate = β̂_ivw,
        se = se_ivw,
        Q_stat = Q,
        heterogeneity_p = heterogeneity_p,
        n_snps = length(Γ̂_GX)
    )
end

# Simulate two-sample MR data
function simulate_twosample_mr(n_snps=20, n1=50000, n2=60000; β_true=0.4)
    # SNP effects on exposure (from exposure GWAS)
    Γ_GX_true = randn(n_snps) * 0.1
    Γ̂_GX = Γ_GX_true + randn(n_snps) * 0.02
    se_GX = fill(0.02, n_snps)
    
    # SNP effects on outcome (from outcome GWAS)
    Γ_GY_true = β_true * Γ_GX_true + randn(n_snps) * 0.02  # Some pleiotropy
    Γ̂_GY = Γ_GY_true + randn(n_snps) * 0.025
    se_GY = fill(0.025, n_snps)
    
    return (Γ̂_GX=Γ̂_GX, Γ̂_GY=Γ̂_GY, se_GX=se_GX, se_GY=se_GY)
end

# Example two-sample MR analysis
gwas_data = simulate_twosample_mr(25, β_true=0.4)
two_sample_result = two_sample_mr(gwas_data.Γ̂_GX, gwas_data.Γ̂_GY, 
                                 gwas_data.se_GX, gwas_data.se_GY)

println("\nTwo-sample MR results:")
println("IVW estimate: $(round(two_sample_result.estimate, digits=3)) ± $(round(two_sample_result.se, digits=3))")
println("Heterogeneity Q: $(round(two_sample_result.Q_stat, digits=2)) (p = $(round(two_sample_result.heterogeneity_p, digits=3)))")
println("Number of instruments: $(two_sample_result.n_snps)")

4.10 Sensitivity Analysis for IV Assumptions

The validity of IV estimates depends critically on the untestable exclusion restriction. Various sensitivity analysis methods have been developed to assess robustness to violations of this assumption.

4.10.1 Testing for Overidentification

When multiple instruments are available, the overidentification test (Sargan-Hansen test) can detect violations of the exclusion restriction, though it cannot identify which specific instruments are invalid.

"""
Sargan-Hansen overidentification test
"""
function overid_test(Y::Vector, T::Vector, Z::Matrix)
    n, k = size(Z)  # k instruments
    p = 1  # Number of endogenous regressors
    
    # 2SLS estimation
    first_stage = Z \ T
    T_hat = Z * first_stage
    second_stage_coeff = [T_hat ones(n)] \ Y
    
    # Residuals
    û = Y - [T_hat ones(n)] * second_stage_coeff
    
    # Test statistic: n * R² from regression of residuals on instruments
    aux_reg = Z \ û
    R_squared = 1 - sum((û - Z * aux_reg).^2) / sum((û .- mean(û)).^2)
    
    test_stat = n * R_squared
    df = k - p  # Degrees of freedom
    
    p_value = 1 - cdf(Chisq(df), test_stat)
    
    return (test_stat=test_stat, p_value=p_value, df=df)
end

4.11 Worked Example: Returns to Education

Consider the classic application of IV methods to estimating returns to education. The challenge is that schooling decisions are endogenous — individuals with higher unobserved ability may choose more education and also earn higher wages, leading to upward bias in OLS estimates.

Angrist and Krueger (1991) proposed using quarter of birth as an instrument, exploiting compulsory schooling laws that create exogenous variation in education. We’ll simulate data mimicking their approach:

"""
Simulate returns to education data
"""
function simulate_education_returns(n=5000)
    # Quarter of birth (instrument)
    # Q1 births face earlier school entry, potentially less education
    quarter = rand(1:4, n)
    Z = Float64.(quarter .== 1)  # Born in Q1
    
    # Unobserved ability
    ability = randn(n)
    
    # Family background
    family_income


\newpage

# Chapter 6: Difference-in-Differences and Synthetic Controls

Difference-in-differences (DiD) and synthetic control methods represent two of the most influential approaches to causal inference in observational studies, particularly when natural experiments create variation in treatment assignment over time. These methods exploit temporal variation in treatment exposure to identify causal effects under weaker assumptions than those required for cross-sectional identification strategies. Both approaches have revolutionized empirical research in economics, epidemiology, and policy evaluation, providing credible causal estimates in settings where randomized experiments are infeasible.

The fundamental insight underlying both methodologies is that comparing outcomes before and after treatment implementation can eliminate time-invariant confounding factors. Difference-in-differences achieves this by comparing the change in outcomes for treated units to the change for untreated units, effectively using the control group to account for secular trends. Synthetic control methods extend this logic by constructing an artificial control unit as a weighted average of donor units, optimally chosen to match the pre-treatment characteristics of the treated unit.

## 6.1 The Difference-in-Differences Framework

### 6.1.1 The Canonical Two-Period, Two-Group Model

The simplest difference-in-differences design involves two time periods and two groups: one that receives treatment between the periods and one that never receives treatment. Let $Y_{it}(d)$ denote the potential outcome for unit $i$ in period $t$ under treatment status $d \in \{0,1\}$. The observed outcome is $Y_{it} = D_{it} Y_{it}(1) + (1-D_{it}) Y_{it}(0)$, where $D_{it}$ is the treatment indicator.

> **Definition 6.1 (Canonical DiD Setup).** A canonical difference-in-differences design consists of:
> - Two time periods: $t \in \{0,1\}$
> - Two groups: treated ($G_i = 1$) and control ($G_i = 0$)
> - Treatment assignment: $D_{it} = G_i \cdot \mathbf{1}[t=1]$
> - No units are treated in period 0, and all treated units receive treatment in period 1

The key identifying assumption is the parallel trends assumption, which requires that in the absence of treatment, the average outcomes for treated and control groups would have followed parallel trajectories.

> **Assumption 6.1 (Parallel Trends).** 
> $$E[Y_{i1}(0) - Y_{i0}(0) | G_i = 1] = E[Y_{i1}(0) - Y_{i0}(0) | G_i = 0]$$

Under this assumption, the average treatment effect on the treated (ATT) is identified as:

> **Theorem 6.1 (DiD Identification).** Under the parallel trends assumption,
> $$\text{ATT} = E[Y_{i1}(1) - Y_{i0}(0) | G_i = 1] = \{E[Y_{i1} | G_i = 1] - E[Y_{i0} | G_i = 1]\} - \{E[Y_{i1} | G_i = 0] - E[Y_{i0} | G_i = 0]\}$$

*Proof.* The ATT can be written as:
$$\text{ATT} = E[Y_{i1}(1) - Y_{i0}(0) | G_i = 1] = E[Y_{i1}(1) | G_i = 1] - E[Y_{i0}(0) | G_i = 1]$$

Since no treated units receive treatment in period 0, we have $Y_{i0} = Y_{i0}(0)$ for $G_i = 1$. The challenge is identifying $E[Y_{i1}(0) | G_i = 1]$, the counterfactual outcome for treated units in period 1 under no treatment.

Under parallel trends:
$$E[Y_{i1}(0) | G_i = 1] = E[Y_{i0}(0) | G_i = 1] + E[Y_{i1}(0) - Y_{i0}(0) | G_i = 0]$$

Since no control units receive treatment in either period, $Y_{it} = Y_{it}(0)$ for $G_i = 0$. Therefore:
$$E[Y_{i1}(0) - Y_{i0}(0) | G_i = 0] = E[Y_{i1} | G_i = 0] - E[Y_{i0} | G_i = 0]$$

Substituting yields the DiD estimand. □

The DiD estimator is typically implemented via the regression:
$$Y_{it} = \alpha + \beta \cdot \text{Treated}_i + \gamma \cdot \text{Post}_t + \delta \cdot \text{Treated}_i \times \text{Post}_t + \varepsilon_{it}$$

where $\delta$ estimates the ATT.

### 6.1.2 Computational Implementation

The following Julia implementation demonstrates DiD estimation with confidence intervals and visualization:

```julia
using DataFrames, GLM, StatsBase, Random, Distributions, CairoMakie

# Generate synthetic DiD data
function generate_did_data(n_treated=100, n_control=100; seed=123)
    Random.seed!(seed)
    
    # Time-invariant unit effects
    α_treated = randn(n_treated)
    α_control = randn(n_control)
    
    # Common time trend
    time_trend = 2.0
    
    # Treatment effect
    treatment_effect = 1.5
    
    # Create data
    data = DataFrame()
    
    # Control group
    for i in 1:n_control
        for t in [0, 1]
            push!(data, (
                unit = i,
                group = 0,
                time = t,
                treated = 0,
                post = t,
                outcome = α_control[i] + time_trend * t + 0.5 * randn()
            ))
        end
    end
    
    # Treatment group
    for i in 1:n_treated
        for t in [0, 1]
            is_treated = t == 1
            push!(data, (
                unit = n_control + i,
                group = 1,
                time = t,
                treated = is_treated ? 1 : 0,
                post = t,
                outcome = α_treated[i] + time_trend * t + 
                         treatment_effect * is_treated + 0.5 * randn()
            ))
        end
    end
    
    return data
end

# DiD estimation function
function estimate_did(data)
    # Create interaction term
    data.treated_post = data.group .* data.post
    
    # Estimate regression
    model = lm(@formula(outcome ~ group + post + treated_post), data)
    
    # Extract DiD coefficient
    did_coef = coef(model)[4]  # treated_post coefficient
    did_se = stderror(model)[4]
    
    # 95% confidence interval
    ci_lower = did_coef - 1.96 * did_se
    ci_upper = did_coef + 1.96 * did_se
    
    return (
        estimate = did_coef,
        std_error = did_se,
        ci_lower = ci_lower,
        ci_upper = ci_upper,
        model = model
    )
end

# Visualize DiD design
function plot_did(data)
    # Calculate group means by period
    group_means = combine(groupby(data, [:group, :time]), :outcome => mean => :mean_outcome)
    
    fig = Figure(size = (600, 400))
    ax = Axis(fig[1, 1], 
              xlabel = "Time Period", 
              ylabel = "Outcome",
              title = "Difference-in-Differences Visualization")
    
    # Plot means
    treated_means = filter(row -> row.group == 1, group_means)
    control_means = filter(row -> row.group == 0, group_means)
    
    lines!(ax, treated_means.time, treated_means.mean_outcome, 
           color = :red, linewidth = 3, label = "Treated")
    lines!(ax, control_means.time, control_means.mean_outcome, 
           color = :blue, linewidth = 3, label = "Control")
    
    # Add markers
    scatter!(ax, treated_means.time, treated_means.mean_outcome, 
             color = :red, markersize = 10)
    scatter!(ax, control_means.time, control_means.mean_outcome, 
             color = :blue, markersize = 10)
    
    # Add vertical line at treatment time
    vlines!(ax, [0.5], color = :gray, linestyle = :dash, alpha = 0.7)
    
    axislegend(ax, position = :lt)
    
    return fig
end

# Example usage
data = generate_did_data()
results = estimate_did(data)
fig = plot_did(data)

println("DiD Estimate: $(round(results.estimate, digits=3))")
println("Standard Error: $(round(results.std_error, digits=3))")
println("95% CI: [$(round(results.ci_lower, digits=3)), $(round(results.ci_upper, digits=3))]")

4.12 6.2 Staggered Adoption and the Crisis of Two-Way Fixed Effects

4.12.1 6.2.1 The Problem with Heterogeneous Treatment Effects

The canonical DiD model becomes problematic when treatment adoption is staggered across units and time periods, and when treatment effects are heterogeneous. The standard two-way fixed effects (TWFE) regression: \[Y_{it} = \alpha_i + \lambda_t + \delta D_{it} + \varepsilon_{it}\]

where \(\alpha_i\) and \(\lambda_t\) are unit and time fixed effects, may not identify a meaningful causal parameter under these conditions.

Theorem 6.2 (Goodman-Bacon Decomposition). In a setting with staggered treatment adoption, the TWFE estimator \(\hat{\delta}\) can be written as a weighted average of all possible 2×2 DiD comparisons: \[\hat{\delta} = \sum_{k,\ell} s_{k,\ell} \hat{\delta}_{k,\ell}\] where \(s_{k,\ell}\) are the weights on each 2×2 comparison between groups \(k\) and \(\ell\), and some comparisons may use already-treated units as controls.

Proof Sketch. The TWFE estimator can be decomposed by considering all possible timing group pairs. Each comparison receives a weight proportional to the variance of the treatment indicator for that pair. Critically, some comparisons use “forbidden” comparisons where already-treated units serve as controls for newly treated units, potentially biasing the estimate if treatment effects evolve over time. □

4.12.2 6.2.2 Modern DiD Methods

Several recent methodological advances address the staggered adoption problem:

Callaway and Sant’Anna (2021) propose group-time average treatment effects (ATT(g,t)) where \(g\) denotes the group (first treatment period) and \(t\) the time period. They estimate these effects separately and then aggregate them.

Sun and Abraham (2021) use an event-study approach with cohort-relative time indicators, avoiding problematic comparisons entirely.

de Chaisemartin and D’Haultfœuille (2020) develop the DID_multiplegt estimator that weights group-time cells appropriately.

4.12.3 6.2.3 Implementation of Modern DiD Methods

using FixedEffectModels, CategoricalArrays

# Generate staggered adoption data
function generate_staggered_data(n_units=50, n_periods=10; seed=456)
    Random.seed!(seed)
    
    data = DataFrame()
    
    for i in 1:n_units
        # Random treatment timing (some never treated)
        treat_time = rand() < 0.3 ? nothing : rand(4:8)
        
        # Unit fixed effect
        α_i = randn()
        
        for t in 1:n_periods
            is_treated = !isnothing(treat_time) && t >= treat_time
            
            # Time-varying treatment effect (grows over time)
            effect = is_treated ? 1.0 + 0.2 * (t - treat_time) : 0.0
            
            push!(data, (
                unit = i,
                period = t,
                treated = is_treated ? 1 : 0,
                treat_group = isnothing(treat_time) ? 0 : treat_time,
                outcome = α_i + 0.1 * t + effect + 0.3 * randn()
            ))
        end
    end
    
    return data
end

# Event study with leads and lags
function event_study(data, max_lead=3, max_lag=5)
    # Create relative time indicators
    data.rel_time = ifelse.(data.treat_group .== 0, missing, 
                           data.period .- data.treat_group)
    
    # Create binned event time dummies
    data.event_time = categorical(data.rel_time)
    
    # Remove base period (t = -1)
    data_reg = filter(row -> ismissing(row.rel_time) || row.rel_time != -1, data)
    
    # Estimate event study regression
    formula = @formula(outcome ~ fe(unit) + fe(period) + event_time)
    model = reg(data_reg, formula, Vcov.cluster(:unit))
    
    return model
end

# Group-time average treatment effects (simplified version)
function group_time_att(data)
    # Focus on treated groups only
    treated_data = filter(row -> row.treat_group > 0, data)
    
    group_time_effects = DataFrame()
    
    for g in unique(treated_data.treat_group)
        group_data = filter(row -> row.treat_group == g, treated_data)
        
        for t in unique(group_data.period)
            if t >= g  # Only post-treatment periods
                # Get treated units in group g at time t
                treated_outcomes = filter(row -> row.period == t && row.treat_group == g, group_data)
                
                # Get never-treated units at time t (as comparison group)
                never_treated = filter(row -> row.treat_group == 0 && row.period == t, data)
                
                if nrow(never_treated) > 0
                    # Simple ATT estimate (this is simplified - proper implementation 
                    # would use more sophisticated matching/weighting)
                    att_gt = mean(treated_outcomes.outcome) - mean(never_treated.outcome)
                    
                    push!(group_time_effects, (
                        group = g,
                        time = t,
                        att = att_gt,
                        n_treated = nrow(treated_outcomes),
                        n_control = nrow(never_treated)
                    ))
                end
            end
        end
    end
    
    return group_time_effects
end

# Example usage
staggered_data = generate_staggered_data()
event_model = event_study(staggered_data)
group_time_atts = group_time_att(staggered_data)

println("Event Study Results:")
println(event_model)
println("\nGroup-Time ATTs (first 10 rows):")
println(first(group_time_atts, 10))

4.13 6.3 Event Study Designs

Event studies extend the DiD framework by examining treatment effects across multiple time periods relative to treatment initiation. This approach is particularly valuable for understanding the dynamics of treatment effects and testing the parallel trends assumption.

4.13.1 6.3.1 The Event Study Model

The event study regression takes the form: \[Y_{it} = \alpha_i + \lambda_t + \sum_{k \neq -1} \beta_k D_{it}^k + \varepsilon_{it}\]

where \(D_{it}^k\) is an indicator for unit \(i\) being \(k\) periods relative to treatment initiation at time \(t\). The coefficient \(k = -1\) is typically omitted as the reference period.

The coefficients \(\{\beta_k\}_{k < 0}\) test the parallel trends assumption—they should be statistically indistinguishable from zero if the assumption holds. The coefficients \(\{\beta_k\}_{k \geq 0}\) trace out the dynamic treatment effects.

4.13.2 6.3.2 Testing Parallel Trends

Definition 6.2 (Pre-trend Test). A pre-trend test examines whether \(H_0: \beta_k = 0\) for all \(k < 0\) in the event study regression.

Common approaches include: 1. Joint F-test of all pre-treatment coefficients 2. Individual t-tests for each pre-treatment coefficient 3. Linear trend test in pre-treatment periods

# Pre-trend testing function
function test_pretrends(event_results, pre_periods)
    # Extract pre-treatment coefficients and standard errors
    # (This is simplified - actual implementation depends on regression package)
    
    pre_coefs = Float64[]
    pre_ses = Float64[]
    
    for k in pre_periods
        coef_name = "event_time: $k"
        if coef_name in names(event_results.coef)
            push!(pre_coefs, event_results.coef[coef_name])
            push!(pre_ses, event_results.se[coef_name])
        end
    end
    
    # Joint test (simplified)
    joint_stat = sum((pre_coefs ./ pre_ses).^2)
    p_value = 1 - cdf(Chisq(length(pre_coefs)), joint_stat)
    
    return (
        individual_pvalues = 2 * (1 .- cdf.(Normal(), abs.(pre_coefs ./ pre_ses))),
        joint_pvalue = p_value,
        joint_statistic = joint_stat
    )
end

4.14 6.4 Synthetic Control Method

The synthetic control method, developed by Abadie and Gardeazabal (2003) and extended by Abadie et al. (2010), provides an alternative approach to causal inference when a single unit (or small number of units) receives treatment, and when suitable control units are not immediately obvious.

4.14.1 6.4.1 The Synthetic Control Framework

Consider \(J+1\) units observed over \(T\) periods, where unit 1 receives treatment at time \(T_0 + 1\) and units \(2, \ldots, J+1\) never receive treatment. The synthetic control method constructs a weighted average of control units that best approximates the pre-treatment characteristics of the treated unit.

Definition 6.3 (Synthetic Control). A synthetic control is a convex combination of donor units: \[\sum_{j=2}^{J+1} w_j^* = 1, \quad w_j^* \geq 0 \quad \forall j\] where the weights \(\mathbf{w}^* = (w_2^*, \ldots, w_{J+1}^*)\) minimize: \[||\mathbf{X}_1 - \mathbf{X}_0 \mathbf{w}||_V = \sqrt{(\mathbf{X}_1 - \mathbf{X}_0 \mathbf{w})' \mathbf{V} (\mathbf{X}_1 - \mathbf{X}_0 \mathbf{w})}\] where \(\mathbf{X}_1\) contains pre-treatment characteristics of the treated unit, \(\mathbf{X}_0\) contains characteristics of donor units, and \(\mathbf{V}\) is a positive semi-definite weighting matrix.

The treatment effect is estimated as the difference between the observed outcome for the treated unit and its synthetic counterpart: \[\hat{\alpha}_{1t} = Y_{1t} - \sum_{j=2}^{J+1} w_j^* Y_{jt}\]

4.14.2 6.4.2 Optimization Problem

The synthetic control weights solve the constrained optimization problem: \[\min_{\mathbf{w}} (\mathbf{X}_1 - \mathbf{X}_0 \mathbf{w})' \mathbf{V} (\mathbf{X}_1 - \mathbf{X}_0 \mathbf{w})\] subject to \(\sum_{j=2}^{J+1} w_j = 1\) and \(w_j \geq 0\) for all \(j\).

The weighting matrix \(\mathbf{V}\) is often chosen to minimize the mean squared prediction error of the outcome variable in pre-treatment periods, though other choices are possible.

4.14.3 6.4.3 Theoretical Justification

Theorem 6.3 (Abadie et al. 2010). Suppose the outcome follows the factor model: \[Y_{jt} = \delta_t D_{jt} + \boldsymbol{\theta}_t' \boldsymbol{\mu}_j + \boldsymbol{\lambda}_t' \boldsymbol{f}_j + \varepsilon_{jt}\] where \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_j\) are observed characteristics, \(\boldsymbol{f}_j\) are unobserved factors, and the synthetic control satisfies: \[\sum_{j=2}^{J+1} w_j^* \boldsymbol{\mu}_j = \boldsymbol{\mu}_1 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{j=2}^{J+1} w_j^* \boldsymbol{f}_j = \boldsymbol{f}_1\] Then the bias of the synthetic control estimator is: \[Y_{1t} - \sum_{j=2}^{J+1} w_j^* Y_{jt} - \delta_t = \sum_{j=2}^{J+1} w_j^* \varepsilon_{jt} - \varepsilon_{1t}\]

This shows that if the synthetic control perfectly matches both observed and unobserved characteristics, the estimator is unbiased up to idiosyncratic shocks.

4.14.4 6.4.4 Implementation of Synthetic Control

using Optimization, OptimizationOptimJL, LinearAlgebra

# Synthetic control implementation
struct SyntheticControl
    treated_unit::Int
    donor_units::Vector{Int}
    treatment_time::Int
    weights::Vector{Float64}
    X1::Matrix{Float64}  # Treated unit characteristics
    X0::Matrix{Float64}  # Donor units characteristics
    Y1_pre::Vector{Float64}  # Treated unit pre-treatment outcomes
    Y0_pre::Matrix{Float64}  # Donor units pre-treatment outcomes
end

function fit_synthetic_control(Y, X, treated_unit, treatment_time; 
                              V_matrix=nothing, predictor_weights=nothing)
    n_units, n_periods = size(Y)
    pre_periods = 1:(treatment_time-1)
    donor_units = setdiff(1:n_units, treated_unit)
    
    # Extract pre-treatment data
    Y1_pre = Y[treated_unit, pre_periods]
    Y0_pre = Y[donor_units, pre_periods]
    X1 = X[treated_unit, :]'
    X0 = X[donor_units, :]'
    
    # Default V matrix (identity)
    if isnothing(V_matrix)
        V_matrix = I(size(X0, 1))
    end
    
    # Objective function: minimize ||X1 - X0*w||_V subject to constraints
    function objective(w, p)
        residual = X1 - X0 * w
        return dot(residual, V_matrix * residual)
    end
    
    # Constraints: weights sum to 1 and are non-negative
    function constraints(res, w, p)
        res[1] = sum(w) - 1.0  # equality constraint
    end
    
    # Initial guess
    n_donors = length(donor_units)
    w0 = fill(1.0 / n_donors, n_donors)
    
    # Set up optimization problem
    prob = OptimizationProblem(objective, w0,
                              lcons = [0.0], ucons = [0.0],  # equality constraint
                              lb = zeros(n_donors),           # non-negativity
                              ub = ones(n_donors))
    
    # Solve
    sol = solve(prob, IPNewton())
    
    return SyntheticControl(
        treated_unit,
        donor_units,
        treatment_time,
        sol.u,
        X1,
        X0,
        Y1_pre,
        Y0_pre
    )
end

# Calculate treatment effects
function treatment_effects(sc::SyntheticControl, Y)
    n_periods = size(Y, 2)
    effects = zeros(n_periods)
    
    for t in 1:n_periods
        synthetic_outcome = sum(sc.weights .* Y[sc.donor_units, t])
        effects[t] = Y[sc.treated_unit, t] - synthetic_outcome
    end
    
    return effects
end

# Generate example data for synthetic control
function generate_synth_data(n_units=20, n_periods=30, treatment_time=21; seed=789)
    Random.seed!(seed)
    
    # Unit characteristics
    X = randn(n_units, 3)  # 3 characteristics
    
    # Outcomes with factor structure
    μ = randn(n_units)  # Unit fixed effects
    λ = randn(n_periods)  # Time effects
    
    Y = zeros(n_units, n_periods)
    
    for i in 1:n_units
        for t in 1:n_periods
            # Factor model outcome
            Y[i, t] = μ[i] + 0.5 * λ[t] + 0.3 * dot(X[i, :], [1.0, 0.5, -0.3]) + 0.2 * randn()
            
            # Add treatment effect for unit 1 after treatment time
            if i == 1 && t >= treatment_time
                Y[i, t] += 2.0 + 0.1 * (t - treatment_time)  # Growing treatment effect
            end
        end
    end
    
    return Y, X
end

# Example usage
Y_synth, X_synth = generate_synth_data()
sc = fit_synthetic_control(Y_synth, X_synth, 1, 21)
effects = treatment_effects(sc, Y_synth)

println("Synthetic Control Weights:")
for (i, w) in enumerate(sc.weights)
    if w > 0.01  # Only show significant weights
        println("  Unit $(sc.donor_units[i]): $(round(w, digits=3))")
    end
end

println("\nPre-treatment RMSPE: $(round(sqrt(mean(effects[1:20].^2)), digits=3))")
println("Average post-treatment effect: $(round(mean(effects[21:end]), digits=3))")

4.15 6.5 Inference for Synthetic Controls

4.15.1 6.5.1 Permutation-Based Inference

Since the synthetic control method typically involves a single treated unit, conventional statistical inference is not immediately applicable. Abadie et al. (2010) propose permutation-based inference that compares the magnitude of the estimated effect to the distribution of “placebo” effects obtained by applying the synthetic control method to each donor unit.

Definition 6.4 (Permutation P-value). The permutation p-value for the synthetic control estimator is: \[p = \frac{1}{J} \sum_{j=2}^{J+1} \mathbf{1}\left[\frac{\text{MSPE}_j^{\text{post}}}{\text{MSPE}_j^{\text{pre}}} \geq \frac{\text{MSPE}_1^{\text{post}}}{\text{MSPE}_1^{\text{pre}}}\right]\] where \(\text{MSPE}_j^{\text{pre}}\) and \(\text{MSPE}_j^{\text{post}}\) are the mean squared prediction errors for unit \(j\) in pre- and post-treatment periods, respectively.

4.15.2 6.5.2 Conformal Inference

Recent work by Chernozhukov et al. (2021) develops conformal prediction intervals for synthetic controls that provide finite-sample coverage guarantees without distributional assumptions.

# Permutation inference for synthetic controls
function permutation_inference(Y, X, treated_unit, treatment_time; n_permutations=nothing)
    n_units, n_periods = size(Y)
    pre_periods = 1:(treatment_time-1)
    post_periods = treatment_time:n_periods
    
    # Use all units if n_permutations not specified
    if isnothing(n_permutations)
        units_to_test = 1:n_units
    else
        units_to_test = sample(1:n_units, min(n_permutations, n_units), replace=false)
    end
    
    mspe_ratios = Float64[]
    
    for unit in units_to_test
        # Fit synthetic control for this unit
        try
            sc = fit_synthetic_control(Y, X, unit, treatment_time)
            effects = treatment_effects(sc, Y)
            
            # Calculate MSPE ratio
            mspe_pre = mean(effects[pre_periods].^2)
            mspe_post = mean(effects[post_periods].^2)
            
            # Avoid division by zero
            if mspe_pre > 1e-10
                push!(mspe_ratios, mspe_post / mspe_pre)
            end
        catch e
            # Skip units that cause optimization issues
            continue
        end
    end
    
    # Calculate p-value
    treated_ratio = mspe_ratios[findfirst(x -> x == treated_unit, units_to_test)]
    p_value = mean(mspe_ratios .>= treated_ratio)
    
    return (
        mspe_ratios = mspe_ratios,
        p_value = p_value,
        treated_ratio = treated_ratio
    )
end

# Visualize permutation results
function plot_permutation_test(inference_results)
    fig = Figure(size = (600, 400))
    ax = Axis(fig[1, 1], 
              xlabel = "MSPE Ratio (Post/Pre)", 
              ylabel = "Density",
              title = "Permutation Test Distribution")
    
    # Histogram of ratios
    hist!(ax, inference_results.mspe_ratios, bins = 20, alpha = 0.7, color = :lightblue)
    
    # Mark treated unit
    vlines!(ax, [inference_results.treated_ratio], 
            color = :red, linewidth = 3, linestyle = :dash,
            label = "Treated Unit")
    
    axislegend(ax)
    
    return fig
end

# Example permutation test
inference_results = permutation_inference(Y_synth, X_synth, 1, 21)
perm_fig = plot_permutation_test(inference_results)

println("Permutation Test Results:")
println("P-value: $(round(inference_results.p_value, digits=3))")
println("Treated unit MSPE ratio: $(round(inference_results.treated_ratio, digits=3))")

4.16 6.6 Extensions and Robustness

4.16.1 6.6.1 Multiple Treated Units

When multiple units receive treatment, the synthetic control method can be extended by:

  1. Averaging approach: Construct synthetic controls for each treated unit and average the effects
  2. Pooled approach: Pool all treated units and construct a single synthetic control
  3. Matrix completion: Use matrix completion methods to impute counterfactual outcomes

4.16.2 6.6.2 Time-Varying Confounders

Both DiD and synthetic controls can be extended to handle time-varying confounders:

  • DiD with covariates: Include time-varying covariates in the regression
  • Interactive fixed effects: Allow for time-varying factor loadings
  • **Synthetic

5 Chapter 7: Regression Discontinuity and Other Quasi-Experimental Designs

Quasi-experimental designs exploit natural experiments or institutional features to identify causal effects when randomized controlled trials are infeasible. Among these designs, regression discontinuity (RD) stands out for its internal validity and transparency. This chapter develops the theoretical foundations of RD designs and extends to related quasi-experimental methods including interrupted time series and bunching designs.

5.1 7.1 The Regression Discontinuity Framework

5.1.1 7.1.1 Sharp Regression Discontinuity

The regression discontinuity design exploits arbitrary cutoff rules that determine treatment assignment. Consider a running variable X and a cutoff c. In the sharp RD design, treatment assignment is a deterministic function of whether X exceeds c.

Definition 7.1 (Sharp Regression Discontinuity Design). A sharp regression discontinuity design is characterized by: 1. A running variable X ∈ ℝ with support containing a cutoff c 2. Treatment assignment D = 1{X ≥ c} 3. Potential outcomes Y(0) and Y(1) that are continuous in expectation at c

The key insight is that near the cutoff, treatment assignment is “as good as random” since individuals just above and below c should be similar in all characteristics except treatment status.

Assumption 7.1 (Continuity at Cutoff). The conditional expectation functions E[Y(0)|X = x] and E[Y(1)|X = x] are continuous at x = c.

Under this assumption, the treatment effect at the cutoff is:

τ = E[Y(1) - Y(0)|X = c] = lim_{x↓c} E[Y|X = x] - lim_{x↑c} E[Y|X = x]

This is the average treatment effect for units at the cutoff, representing a local average treatment effect (LATE).

Theorem 7.1 (RD Identification). Under Assumption 7.1, the treatment effect at the cutoff is identified as: τ = lim_{x→c⁺} E[Y|X = x] - lim_{x→c⁻} E[Y|X = x]

Proof. For x ≥ c, all units receive treatment, so E[Y|X = x] = E[Y(1)|X = x]. For x < c, no units receive treatment, so E[Y|X = x] = E[Y(0)|X = x]. By continuity: - lim_{x→c⁺} E[Y|X = x] = lim_{x→c⁺} E[Y(1)|X = x] = E[Y(1)|X = c] - lim_{x→c⁻} E[Y|X = x] = lim_{x→c⁻} E[Y(0)|X = x] = E[Y(0)|X = c]

Therefore, τ = E[Y(1)|X = c] - E[Y(0)|X = c] = E[Y(1) - Y(0)|X = c]. □

5.1.2 7.1.2 Fuzzy Regression Discontinuity

In practice, compliance with the cutoff rule may be imperfect. The fuzzy RD design accommodates situations where the cutoff increases the probability of treatment but does not guarantee it.

Definition 7.2 (Fuzzy Regression Discontinuity Design). A fuzzy regression discontinuity design is characterized by: 1. A running variable X with cutoff c 2. A discontinuous jump in treatment probability P(D = 1|X) at c 3. Monotonicity: D(1) ≥ D(0) for all units, where D(z) is the treatment received when the cutoff rule assigns treatment z

In fuzzy RD, we identify the local average treatment effect for compliers using instrumental variables methods, treating the cutoff rule as an instrument.

Theorem 7.2 (Fuzzy RD Identification). Under standard IV assumptions plus continuity, the LATE for compliers at the cutoff is: τ_{LATE} =

The denominator represents the first-stage jump in treatment probability, while the numerator gives the reduced-form effect on outcomes.

5.2 7.2 Local Polynomial Estimation

5.2.1 7.2.1 Theoretical Framework

The regression discontinuity estimator requires estimating the conditional expectation function E[Y|X = x] on both sides of the cutoff. Local polynomial regression provides a flexible, nonparametric approach.

Consider the model Y = μ(X) + ε where μ(·) is smooth except at c. We estimate μ(c⁻) and μ(c⁺) using local polynomials of order p:

μ_{-}(x) = β₀ + β₁(x - c) + … + βₚ(x - c)ᵖ for x < c μ_{+}(x) = γ₀ + γ₁(x - c) + … + γₚ(x - c)ᵖ for x ≥ c

The local polynomial estimators minimize weighted least squares objectives within bandwidth h of the cutoff.

Definition 7.3 (Local Polynomial RD Estimator). The pth-order local polynomial RD estimator is: τ̂ = γ̂₀ - β̂₀ where β̂₀ and γ̂₀ solve the weighted least squares problems for observations within bandwidth h of c.

5.2.2 7.2.2 Bandwidth Selection

Bandwidth selection involves a bias-variance tradeoff. The MSE-optimal bandwidth balances the squared bias from undersmoothing against the variance from including too few observations.

Theorem 7.3 (MSE-Optimal Bandwidth). For local linear regression (p = 1), the MSE-optimal bandwidth is: h_{opt} = C · n^{-1/5} where C depends on the density of X at c, the conditional variance of Y, and derivatives of the regression function.

The Imbens-Kalyanaraman (2012) procedure provides a data-driven method for selecting h_{opt}:

using GLM, DataFrames, Statistics, StatsBase

function ik_bandwidth(X, Y, c; kernel="triangular")
    """
    Imbens-Kalyanaraman optimal bandwidth selection
    """
    n = length(X)
    
    # Step 1: Estimate derivatives using global polynomial
    # Use observations within ±h₁ of cutoff for pilot estimates
    h1 = 1.84 * std(X) * n^(-1/5)
    
    pilot_sample = abs.(X .- c) .<= h1
    X_pilot = X[pilot_sample]
    Y_pilot = Y[pilot_sample]
    
    # Fit 3rd order polynomials on each side
    left_data = (X_pilot .< c) .& pilot_sample[pilot_sample]
    right_data = (X_pilot .>= c) .& pilot_sample[pilot_sample]
    
    if sum(left_data) < 5 || sum(right_data) < 5
        return h1  # fallback if insufficient data
    end
    
    # Estimate second derivatives
    X_left = X_pilot[left_data] .- c
    Y_left = Y_pilot[left_data]
    X_right = X_pilot[right_data] .- c  
    Y_right = Y_pilot[right_data]
    
    # Fit cubic polynomials
    poly_left = lm(@formula(Y ~ X + I(X^2) + I(X^3)), 
                   DataFrame(Y=Y_left, X=X_left))
    poly_right = lm(@formula(Y ~ X + I(X^2) + I(X^3)), 
                    DataFrame(Y=Y_right, X=X_right))
    
    # Extract second derivatives (coefficient of X^2 term × 2)
    m2_left = 2 * coef(poly_left)[3] 
    m2_right = 2 * coef(poly_right)[3]
    
    # Step 2: Estimate conditional variances
    resid_left = residuals(poly_left)
    resid_right = residuals(poly_right)
    
    σ²_left = var(resid_left)
    σ²_right = var(resid_right)
    
    # Step 3: Estimate density at cutoff
    f_c = sum(abs.(X .- c) .<= h1) / (2 * h1 * n)
    
    # Step 4: Calculate regularization constants
    if kernel == "triangular"
        C_K = 3.4375
        C_σ = 2.705
    else  # uniform kernel
        C_K = 2.0
        C_σ = 1.5
    end
    
    # Step 5: Compute optimal bandwidth
    numerator = C_σ * (σ²_left + σ²_right)
    denominator = C_K * f_c * (m2_right - m2_left)^2
    
    h_opt = (numerator / denominator)^(1/5) * n^(-1/5)
    
    return max(h_opt, 0.1 * std(X))  # ensure minimum bandwidth
end

The Calonico, Cattaneo, and Titiunik (2014) refinement accounts for bias in the variance estimation:

function cct_bandwidth(X, Y, c; p=1, deriv=0, kernel="triangular")
    """
    Calonico-Cattaneo-Titiunik MSE-optimal bandwidth with bias correction
    """
    n = length(X)
    
    # Initial IK bandwidth
    h_ik = ik_bandwidth(X, Y, c; kernel=kernel)
    
    # Estimate bias correction term
    X_norm = (X .- c) ./ h_ik
    weights = triangular_kernel.(abs.(X_norm))
    
    # Local polynomial regression for bias estimation
    left_idx = (X .< c) .& (abs.(X .- c) .<= h_ik)
    right_idx = (X .>= c) .& (abs.(X .- c) .<= h_ik)
    
    # Estimate higher-order derivatives for bias correction
    # This is a simplified implementation
    h_cct = h_ik * (1 + 0.16)^(-1/5)  # approximation of bias correction
    
    return h_cct
end

function triangular_kernel(u)
    return (abs(u) <= 1.0) ? (1.0 - abs(u)) : 0.0
end

5.2.3 7.2.3 Implementation and Inference

The following code implements the complete RD estimation procedure:

function rd_estimate(X, Y, c; h=nothing, p=1, kernel="triangular", 
                     robust=true, cluster=nothing)
    """
    Estimate regression discontinuity effect using local polynomial regression
    """
    n = length(X)
    
    # Select bandwidth if not provided
    if h === nothing
        h = cct_bandwidth(X, Y, c; p=p, kernel=kernel)
    end
    
    # Create analysis sample within bandwidth
    sample_idx = abs.(X .- c) .<= h
    X_sample = X[sample_idx]
    Y_sample = Y[sample_idx]
    n_sample = length(X_sample)
    
    # Normalize running variable
    X_norm = (X_sample .- c) ./ h
    
    # Create treatment indicator and interaction terms
    treat = X_sample .>= c
    X_center = X_sample .- c
    
    # Build design matrix for local polynomial
    # Include polynomials up to order p, separately for each side
    design_matrix = ones(n_sample, 2 + 2*p)
    design_matrix[:, 1] = treat  # treatment indicator
    
    for j in 1:p
        design_matrix[:, 1+j] = X_center.^j  # left side polynomial
        design_matrix[:, 1+p+j] = treat .* X_center.^j  # right side polynomial
    end
    
    # Apply kernel weights
    if kernel == "triangular"
        weights = triangular_kernel.(abs.(X_norm))
    elseif kernel == "uniform"
        weights = (abs.(X_norm) .<= 1.0) * 1.0
    else
        weights = ones(n_sample)  # no weighting
    end
    
    # Weighted least squares
    W = Diagonal(weights)
    β = (design_matrix' * W * design_matrix) \ (design_matrix' * W * Y_sample)
    
    # RD effect is coefficient on treatment indicator
    τ_hat = β[1]
    
    # Calculate standard errors
    if robust
        # Residuals and meat of sandwich estimator
        residuals = Y_sample - design_matrix * β
        X_weighted = sqrt.(weights) .* design_matrix
        
        if cluster === nothing
            # Heteroskedasticity-robust (HC1)
            Ω = Diagonal(residuals.^2)
            meat = X_weighted' * Ω * X_weighted
        else
            # Cluster-robust (not implemented here for brevity)
            error("Cluster-robust SE not implemented")
        end
        
        # Bread of sandwich
        bread_inv = design_matrix' * W * design_matrix
        vcv = inv(bread_inv) * meat * inv(bread_inv)
        se_τ = sqrt(vcv[1,1])
    else
        # Homoskedastic standard errors
        residuals = Y_sample - design_matrix * β
        σ² = sum(weights .* residuals.^2) / (n_sample - size(design_matrix,2))
        vcv = σ² * inv(design_matrix' * W * design_matrix)
        se_τ = sqrt(vcv[1,1])
    end
    
    # Effective sample sizes
    n_left = sum(sample_idx .& (X .< c))
    n_right = sum(sample_idx .& (X .>= c))
    
    return (
        τ_hat = τ_hat,
        se = se_τ,
        pvalue = 2 * (1 - cdf(Normal(), abs(τ_hat / se_τ))),
        ci_lower = τ_hat - 1.96 * se_τ,
        ci_upper = τ_hat + 1.96 * se_τ,
        bandwidth = h,
        n_eff_left = n_left,
        n_eff_right = n_right,
        n_total = n_sample
    )
end

5.3 7.3 Manipulation Testing and Validation

5.3.1 7.3.1 McCrary Density Test

A fundamental threat to RD validity is precise manipulation of the running variable. If agents can precisely control their position relative to the cutoff, the “as good as random” assumption fails.

Definition 7.4 (McCrary Test). The McCrary (2008) density test examines whether the density of the running variable is continuous at the cutoff by testing: H₀: lim_{x→c⁺} f(x) = lim_{x→c⁻} f(x) against the alternative of a discrete jump in density.

The test statistic is the log ratio of density estimates on each side of the cutoff:

T = ln(f̂₊(c)) - ln(f̂₋(c))
