Under the null hypothesis, T is asymptotically normal with variance that can be estimated from the local linear regression used to estimate the densities.

using KernelDensity

function mccrary_test(X, c; h=nothing, robust=true)
    """
    McCrary (2008) manipulation test for density discontinuity at cutoff
    """
    n = length(X)
    
    # Select bandwidth using rule of thumb if not provided
    if h === nothing
        h = 1.84 * std(X) * n^(-1/5)
    end
    
    # Create histogram bins
    bin_width = h / 3
    bins = (minimum(X)-h):(bin_width):(maximum(X)+h)
    bin_centers = (bins[1:end-1] + bins[2:end]) / 2
    
    # Count observations in each bin
    counts = fit(Histogram, X, bins).weights
    
    # Keep bins within bandwidth of cutoff
    cutoff_bin = argmin(abs.(bin_centers .- c))
    bandwidth_bins = abs.(bin_centers .- c) .<= h
    
    centers_local = bin_centers[bandwidth_bins]
    counts_local = counts[bandwidth_bins]
    
    # Convert counts to densities
    densities = counts_local ./ (n * bin_width)
    
    # Separate left and right sides
    left_side = centers_local .< c
    right_side = centers_local .>= c
    
    if sum(left_side) < 3 || sum(right_side) < 3
        @warn "Insufficient bins for density estimation"
        return (test_stat = NaN, pvalue = NaN, density_left = NaN, density_right = NaN)
    end
    
    # Local linear regression of log density on each side
    X_left = centers_local[left_side] .- c
    Y_left = log.(densities[left_side] .+ 1e-8)  # add small constant
    
    X_right = centers_local[right_side] .- c
    Y_right = log.(densities[right_side] .+ 1e-8)
    
    # Fit local linear regressions
    try
        lm_left = lm(hcat(ones(length(X_left)), X_left), Y_left)
        lm_right = lm(hcat(ones(length(X_right)), X_right), Y_right)
        
        # Extract intercepts (log density at cutoff)
        log_f_left = coef(lm_left)[1]
        log_f_right = coef(lm_right)[1]
        
        # Test statistic
        θ = log_f_right - log_f_left
        
        # Standard error (approximate)
        se_left = sqrt(vcov(lm_left)[1,1])
        se_right = sqrt(vcov(lm_right)[1,1])
        se_θ = sqrt(se_left^2 + se_right^2)
        
        # Test statistic and p-value
        z_stat = θ / se_θ
        pvalue = 2 * (1 - cdf(Normal(), abs(z_stat)))
        
        return (
            test_stat = z_stat,
            pvalue = pvalue,
            log_difference = θ,
            density_left = exp(log_f_left),
            density_right = exp(log_f_right),
            se = se_θ
        )
        
    catch e
        @warn "Error in density regression: $e"
        return (test_stat = NaN, pvalue = NaN, density_left = NaN, density_right = NaN)
    end
end

5.3.2 7.3.2 Cattaneo-Jansson-Ma Test

The Cattaneo, Jansson, and Ma (2018) refinement provides better finite-sample properties and robust inference:

function cjm_test(X, c; h=nothing, p=1, kernel="triangular")
    """
    Cattaneo-Jansson-Ma manipulation test with robust inference
    """
    n = length(X)
    
    if h === nothing
        # Data-driven bandwidth selection for density estimation
        h = 2.0 * std(X) * n^(-1/5)
    end
    
    # Sample within bandwidth
    sample_idx = abs.(X .- c) .<= h
    X_sample = X[sample_idx]
    n_sample = length(X_sample)
    
    # Create pseudo-outcomes (indicator for being observed)
    Y_pseudo = ones(n_sample)
    
    # Use RD estimator on the pseudo-outcomes
    # This estimates the jump in density at cutoff
    rd_result = rd_estimate(X_sample, Y_pseudo, c; h=h, p=p, kernel=kernel)
    
    # Scale by bandwidth to get density discontinuity
    density_jump = rd_result.τ_hat / h
    density_se = rd_result.se / h
    
    # Test statistic
    t_stat = density_jump / density_se
    pvalue = 2 * (1 - cdf(Normal(), abs(t_stat)))
    
    return (
        test_stat = t_stat,
        pvalue = pvalue,
        density_jump = density_jump,
        se = density_se,
        bandwidth = h
    )
end

5.3.3 7.3.3 Covariate Balance Tests

RD validity also requires that predetermined covariates be continuous at the cutoff. We test this for each covariate Z:

function covariate_balance_test(X, Z, c; h=nothing)
    """
    Test for covariate balance at RD cutoff
    Returns RD estimates treating each covariate as outcome
    """
    covariates = isa(Z, Matrix) ? Z : reshape(Z, :, 1)
    n_cov = size(covariates, 2)
    
    results = []
    
    for j in 1:n_cov
        cov_result = rd_estimate(X, covariates[:, j], c; h=h)
        push!(results, (
            covariate = j,
            effect = cov_result.τ_hat,
            se = cov_result.se,
            pvalue = cov_result.pvalue,
            significant = cov_result.pvalue < 0.05
        ))
    end
    
    return results
end

5.4 7.4 Geographic Regression Discontinuity

Geographic RD designs exploit spatial discontinuities in treatment assignment, such as administrative boundaries or geographic features. The main challenge is modeling the spatial structure appropriately.

Consider treatment assignment that varies discontinuously across a geographic boundary B. Let s denote spatial coordinates and d(s, B) be the distance from location s to boundary B.

Definition 7.5 (Geographic RD Design). A geographic regression discontinuity design has: 1. Spatial coordinates s ∈ ℝ² 2. Treatment assignment D = 1{d(s, B) ≥ 0} for boundary B 3. Continuity of potential outcomes across the boundary

The main identification challenge is controlling for smooth spatial trends while isolating the discontinuous effect at the boundary.

using Distances

function geographic_rd(coords, Y, boundary_func; h=nothing, 
                      polynomial_order=1, include_lat_lon=true)
    """
    Geographic regression discontinuity estimation
    coords: n×2 matrix of (longitude, latitude)  
    boundary_func: function that returns signed distance to boundary
    """
    n = size(coords, 1)
    
    # Calculate distance to boundary for each observation
    distances = [boundary_func(coords[i, :]) for i in 1:n]
    
    # Select bandwidth if not provided
    if h === nothing
        h = std(distances) * n^(-1/5)
    end
    
    # Analysis sample within bandwidth of boundary
    sample_idx = abs.(distances) .<= h
    
    if sum(sample_idx) < 20
        error("Insufficient observations near boundary")
    end
    
    coords_sample = coords[sample_idx, :]
    Y_sample = Y[sample_idx]
    dist_sample = distances[sample_idx]
    
    # Treatment indicator
    treat = dist_sample .>= 0
    
    # Build design matrix
    n_sample = length(Y_sample)
    
    # Start with treatment and distance terms
    n_vars = 2 + 2 * polynomial_order
    if include_lat_lon
        n_vars += 4  # linear terms in lat/lon for each side
    end
    
    design_matrix = zeros(n_sample, n_vars)
    design_matrix[:, 1] = treat  # treatment indicator
    
    col_idx = 2
    
    # Distance polynomials (separate for each side)
    for p in 1:polynomial_order
        design_matrix[:, col_idx] = dist_sample.^p  # left side
        design_matrix[:, col_idx + polynomial_order] = treat .* dist_sample.^p  # right side
        col_idx += 1
    end
    col_idx += polynomial_order
    
    # Geographic coordinates (if requested)
    if include_lat_lon
        lon_centered = coords_sample[:, 1] .- mean(coords_sample[:, 1])
        lat_centered = coords_sample[:, 2] .- mean(coords_sample[:, 2])
        
        design_matrix[:, col_idx:(col_idx+1)] = [lon_centered lat_centered]
        design_matrix[:, (col_idx+2):(col_idx+3)] = [treat .* lon_centered treat .* lat_centered]
    end
    
    # Kernel weights based on distance to boundary
    weights = triangular_kernel.(abs.(dist_sample) ./ h)
    
    # Weighted least squares
    W = Diagonal(weights)
    β = (design_matrix' * W * design_matrix) \ (design_matrix' * W * Y_sample)
    
    # RD effect is coefficient on treatment
    τ_hat = β[1]
    
    # Robust standard errors
    residuals = Y_sample - design_matrix * β
    Ω = Diagonal(residuals.^2)
    X_weighted = sqrt.(weights) .* design_matrix
    meat = X_weighted' * Ω * X_weighted
    bread_inv = design_matrix' * W * design_matrix
    vcv = inv(bread_inv) * meat * inv(bread_inv)
    se_τ = sqrt(vcv[1,1])
    
    return (
        τ_hat = τ_hat,
        se = se_τ,
        pvalue = 2 * (1 - cdf(Normal(), abs(τ_hat / se_τ))),
        bandwidth = h,
        n_left = sum(sample_idx .& (distances .< 0)),
        n_right = sum(sample_idx .& (distances .>= 0))
    )
end

5.5 7.5 Bunching Designs

Bunching designs detect behavioral responses to discontinuous incentives by examining the distribution of the running variable itself. When agents respond to a kink or notch in incentives at cutoff c, the distribution of X will exhibit excess mass (bunching) or missing mass.

Definition 7.6 (Bunching Design). A bunching design identifies behavioral responses by comparing the observed density of the running variable to a smooth counterfactual density that would arise absent the discontinuous incentive.

The bunching estimator compares observed frequencies in bins around the cutoff to predicted frequencies from a smooth polynomial fitted to bins away from the cutoff region.

function bunching_estimator(X, c; bunching_window=1.0, 
                           exclude_window=2.0, polynomial_order=3)
    """
    Estimate bunching at a cutoff using excluded region method
    """
    n = length(X)
    
    # Create histogram
    bin_width = (maximum(X) - minimum(X)) / 100
    bins = minimum(X):bin_width:(maximum(X)+bin_width)
    bin_centers = (bins[1:end-1] + bins[2:end]) / 2
    counts = fit(Histogram, X, bins).weights
    
    # Identify regions
    bunching_bins = abs.(bin_centers .- c) .<= bunching_window
    exclude_bins = abs.(bin_centers .- c) .<= exclude_window
    
    # Fitting region: not in excluded window
    fit_bins = .!exclude_bins
    
    if sum(fit_bins) < 2 * polynomial_order + 5
        error("Insufficient bins for polynomial fitting")
    end
    
    # Distance from cutoff for polynomial
    dist_from_cutoff = bin_centers .- c
    
    # Fit polynomial to bins outside excluded region
    design_matrix = zeros(sum(fit_bins), polynomial_order + 1)
    for p in 0:polynomial_order
        design_matrix[:, p+1] = dist_from_cutoff[fit_bins].^p
    end
    
    counts_fit = counts[fit_bins]
    β = design_matrix \ counts_fit
    
    # Predict counterfactual counts in bunching region
    bunching_centers = bin_centers[bunching_bins]
    bunching_design = zeros(length(bunching_centers), polynomial_order + 1)
    for p in 0:polynomial_order
        bunching_design[:, p+1] = (bunching_centers .- c).^p
    end
    
    predicted_counts = bunching_design * β
    observed_counts = counts[bunching_bins]
    
    # Bunching mass
    excess_mass = sum(observed_counts - predicted_counts)
    
    # Bootstrap standard errors
    n_bootstrap = 500
    bootstrap_estimates = zeros(n_bootstrap)
    
    for b in 1:n_bootstrap
        # Resample observations
        X_boot = sample(X, n; replace=true)
        counts_boot = fit(Histogram, X_boot, bins).weights
        
        # Refit polynomial
        β_boot = design_matrix \ counts_boot[fit_bins]
        predicted_boot = bunching_design * β_boot
        
        # Bootstrap bunching estimate
        bootstrap_estimates[b] = sum(counts_boot[bunching_bins] - predicted_boot)
    end
    
    se_bunching = std(bootstrap_estimates)
    
    return (
        excess_mass = excess_mass,
        se = se_bunching,
        pvalue = 2 * (1 - cdf(Normal(), abs(excess_mass / se_bunching))),
        observed_counts = observed_counts,
        predicted_counts = predicted_counts,
        bin_centers = bunching_centers
    )
end

5.6 7.6 Interrupted Time Series

Interrupted time series (ITS) designs exploit discrete changes in policy or treatment at specific time points. Unlike simple before-after comparisons, ITS models the underlying time trend and identifies the treatment effect as a level shift or trend break.

Definition 7.7 (Interrupted Time Series Design). An interrupted time series design has: 1. A time series of outcomes Y₁, Y₂, …, Yₙ 2. A treatment that begins at time T₀
3. Pre-treatment periods t = 1, …, T₀-1 and post-treatment periods t = T₀, …, n

The basic ITS model is:

Yₜ = β₀ + β₁t + β₂D₍t≥T₀₎ + β₃D₍t≥T₀₎(t - T₀) + εₜ

where β₂ captures the immediate level change and β₃ captures the change in trend.

using GLM, TimeSeries

function its_analysis(Y, treatment_time; 
                     pre_trend_test=true, 
                     autocorr_robust=true,
                     seasonal_periods=nothing)
    """
    Interrupted Time Series analysis with trend testing and robust inference
    """
    T = length(Y)
    time = 1:T
    
    # Treatment indicator and time since treatment
    post_treatment = time .>= treatment_time
    time_since = max.(time .- treatment_time .+ 1, 0)
    
    # Basic ITS regression
    df = DataFrame(
        Y = Y,
        time = time,
        post = post_


\newpage

# Chapter 8: Mediation Analysis and Path-Specific Effects

In many scientific investigations, researchers seek to understand not merely whether a treatment affects an outcome, but *how* it affects the outcome. The mechanisms through which treatments operate are often as important as the magnitude of their effects. Mediation analysis provides a formal framework for decomposing causal effects into components that operate through specified intermediate variables, called mediators.

Consider a clinical trial evaluating the effect of a behavioral intervention on cardiovascular health outcomes. While the total effect of the intervention may be positive, understanding whether this effect operates primarily through increased physical activity, improved diet, or reduced stress can inform the design of more targeted interventions. Mediation analysis allows us to quantify these pathway-specific effects rigorously.

This chapter develops the theoretical foundations of causal mediation analysis within both the potential outcomes framework and structural causal models. We examine the identification conditions required for mediation effects, explore computational methods for estimation, and address the challenges posed by treatment-mediator interactions and unmeasured confounding.

## 8.1 Conceptual Framework and Notation

### 8.1.1 The Mediation Setting

In the classical mediation framework, we consider three variables of primary interest:
- Treatment A (often binary, A ∈ {0,1})  
- Mediator M (may be continuous or discrete)
- Outcome Y (may be continuous or discrete)

Additionally, we observe a set of pre-treatment covariates L that may confound the relationships between these variables.

> **Definition 8.1 (Mediation Model).** A mediation model consists of:
> 1. A treatment assignment mechanism for A given L
> 2. A mediator model relating M to A and L  
> 3. An outcome model relating Y to A, M, and L

The fundamental insight of mediation analysis is that the total effect of A on Y can be decomposed into effects that operate through different pathways. The *direct effect* captures the influence of A on Y not mediated by M, while the *indirect effect* captures the influence of A on Y that operates through changes in M.

### 8.1.2 Potential Outcomes for Mediation

To formalize mediation effects, we extend the potential outcomes framework to accommodate mediating variables. Let Y(a,m) denote the potential outcome that would be observed if treatment were set to level a and the mediator were set to level m. Similarly, let M(a) denote the potential mediator value under treatment level a.

The observed outcome and mediator are:
- Y = Y(A,M) = ∑_{a,m} Y(a,m) · I(A=a, M=m)
- M = M(A) = ∑_a M(a) · I(A=a)

This notation allows us to define "cross-world" counterfactuals of the form Y(a, M(a')), representing the outcome under treatment level a with the mediator value that would arise under treatment level a'.

> **Definition 8.2 (Natural Direct and Indirect Effects).** For binary treatment (a,a' ∈ {0,1}), the natural direct and indirect effects are:
> - **Natural Direct Effect (NDE)**: E[Y(1,M(0)) - Y(0,M(0))]
> - **Natural Indirect Effect (NIE)**: E[Y(1,M(1)) - Y(1,M(0))]
> - **Total Effect (TE)**: E[Y(1,M(1)) - Y(0,M(0))] = NDE + NIE

The natural direct effect compares outcomes under treatment and control while holding the mediator fixed at its "natural" level under control. The natural indirect effect compares outcomes under treatment with the mediator at its natural levels under treatment versus control.

### 8.1.3 Controlled Direct Effects

An alternative approach considers *controlled direct effects*, where the mediator is set to a fixed level m rather than its natural level under some treatment condition.

> **Definition 8.3 (Controlled Direct Effect).** The controlled direct effect at mediator level m is:
> **CDE(m)** = E[Y(1,m) - Y(0,m)]

Controlled direct effects are generally easier to identify than natural effects, as they do not require cross-world counterfactuals. However, they may be less interpretable when the chosen level m is not realistic under both treatment conditions.

## 8.2 Identification of Mediation Effects

### 8.2.1 The Sequential Ignorability Assumption

The identification of natural direct and indirect effects requires strong assumptions about unobserved confounding.

> **Assumption 8.1 (Sequential Ignorability).** 
> 1. (Y(a,m), M(a)) ⊥ A | L  (no unmeasured treatment-outcome confounding)
> 2. Y(a,m) ⊥ M | A, L  (no unmeasured mediator-outcome confounding)

The first condition is the standard ignorability assumption for treatment assignment. The second condition requires that, conditional on treatment and covariates, there is no unmeasured confounding of the mediator-outcome relationship.

Sequential ignorability is a strong assumption that is often violated in practice. Unmeasured variables that affect both the mediator and outcome can bias mediation effect estimates even when treatment assignment is randomized.

> **Theorem 8.1 (Identification under Sequential Ignorability).** Under sequential ignorability and positivity conditions, the natural direct and indirect effects are identified by:
> 
> **NIE** = ∫ [E[Y|A=1,M=m,L=l] - E[Y|A=0,M=m,L=l]] × [dP(M=m|A=1,L=l) - dP(M=m|A=0,L=l)] dP(L=l)
>
> **NDE** = ∫ [E[Y|A=1,M=m,L=l] - E[Y|A=0,M=m,L=l]] dP(M=m|A=0,L=l) dP(L=l)

*Proof.* The identification follows from the law of total expectation and sequential ignorability. For the NIE:

NIE = E[Y(1,M(1)) - Y(1,M(0))]
    = E[E[Y(1,M(1)) - Y(1,M(0))|L]]
    = E[E[Y(1,M(1))|L] - E[Y(1,M(0))|L]]

By sequential ignorability:
E[Y(1,M(1))|L] = ∫ E[Y|A=1,M=m,L] dP(M(1)=m|L)
                = ∫ E[Y|A=1,M=m,L] dP(M=m|A=1,L)

Similarly, E[Y(1,M(0))|L] = ∫ E[Y|A=1,M=m,L] dP(M=m|A=0,L).

The result follows by taking differences and integrating over L. The NDE identification is analogous. □

### 8.2.2 Product and Difference Methods

Two computational approaches emerge from the identification formulas: the product method and the difference method.

**Product Method**: Fits separate models for the outcome and mediator, then combines predictions to estimate mediation effects. This approach directly implements the identification formulas.

**Difference Method**: Compares the total effect with and without adjustment for the mediator. The difference between these estimates provides an estimate of the indirect effect.

The product method tends to be more efficient when models are correctly specified, while the difference method can be more robust to certain forms of model misspecification.

## 8.3 Computational Implementation

```julia
using CausalInference, GLM, DataFrames, Distributions, Statistics
using StatsModels, LinearAlgebra, Random

# Mediation analysis implementation
struct MediationAnalysis{T<:Real}
    nde::T          # Natural direct effect
    nie::T          # Natural indirect effect  
    te::T           # Total effect
    nde_se::T       # Standard error of NDE
    nie_se::T       # Standard error of NIE
    te_se::T        # Standard error of TE
end

function Base.show(io::IO, ma::MediationAnalysis)
    println(io, "Mediation Analysis Results:")
    println(io, "  Natural Direct Effect: $(round(ma.nde, digits=4)) (SE: $(round(ma.nde_se, digits=4)))")
    println(io, "  Natural Indirect Effect: $(round(ma.nie, digits=4)) (SE: $(round(ma.nie_se, digits=4)))")
    println(io, "  Total Effect: $(round(ma.te, digits=4)) (SE: $(round(ma.te_se, digits=4)))")
    println(io, "  Proportion Mediated: $(round(ma.nie/ma.te, digits=4))")
end

function mediation_analysis(data::DataFrame, 
                          treatment::Symbol, 
                          mediator::Symbol, 
                          outcome::Symbol,
                          covariates::Vector{Symbol}=Symbol[];
                          n_bootstrap::Int=1000)
    
    # Fit mediator model (treatment + covariates -> mediator)
    med_formula = term(mediator) ~ term(treatment) + sum(term.(covariates))
    med_model = lm(med_formula, data)
    
    # Fit outcome model (treatment + mediator + covariates -> outcome) 
    out_formula = term(outcome) ~ term(treatment) + term(mediator) + sum(term.(covariates))
    out_model = lm(out_formula, data)
    
    # Compute point estimates using product method
    effects = compute_mediation_effects(data, med_model, out_model, 
                                      treatment, mediator, outcome, covariates)
    
    # Bootstrap for confidence intervals
    boot_effects = bootstrap_mediation(data, med_formula, out_formula,
                                     treatment, mediator, outcome, covariates, 
                                     n_bootstrap)
    
    # Compute standard errors
    nde_se = std(boot_effects.nde)
    nie_se = std(boot_effects.nie) 
    te_se = std(boot_effects.te)
    
    return MediationAnalysis(effects.nde, effects.nie, effects.te,
                           nde_se, nie_se, te_se)
end

function compute_mediation_effects(data, med_model, out_model, 
                                 treatment, mediator, outcome, covariates)
    n = nrow(data)
    
    # Create counterfactual datasets
    data_a1 = copy(data)
    data_a0 = copy(data)
    data_a1[!, treatment] .= 1
    data_a0[!, treatment] .= 0
    
    # Predict mediator under each treatment
    m1_pred = predict(med_model, data_a1)  # M(1)
    m0_pred = predict(med_model, data_a0)  # M(0)
    
    # Create datasets for cross-world counterfactuals
    # Y(1, M(1))
    data_y1m1 = copy(data)
    data_y1m1[!, treatment] .= 1
    data_y1m1[!, mediator] = m1_pred
    y1m1_pred = predict(out_model, data_y1m1)
    
    # Y(1, M(0)) 
    data_y1m0 = copy(data)
    data_y1m0[!, treatment] .= 1
    data_y1m0[!, mediator] = m0_pred
    y1m0_pred = predict(out_model, data_y1m0)
    
    # Y(0, M(0))
    data_y0m0 = copy(data)
    data_y0m0[!, treatment] .= 0
    data_y0m0[!, mediator] = m0_pred
    y0m0_pred = predict(out_model, data_y0m0)
    
    # Compute effects
    nde = mean(y1m0_pred - y0m0_pred)           # Natural direct effect
    nie = mean(y1m1_pred - y1m0_pred)           # Natural indirect effect  
    te = mean(y1m1_pred - y0m0_pred)            # Total effect
    
    return (nde=nde, nie=nie, te=te)
end

function bootstrap_mediation(data, med_formula, out_formula,
                           treatment, mediator, outcome, covariates, n_boot)
    n = nrow(data)
    boot_nde = Float64[]
    boot_nie = Float64[]
    boot_te = Float64[]
    
    for i in 1:n_boot
        # Bootstrap sample
        boot_indices = sample(1:n, n, replace=true)
        boot_data = data[boot_indices, :]
        
        # Fit models on bootstrap sample
        med_model = lm(med_formula, boot_data)
        out_model = lm(out_formula, boot_data) 
        
        # Compute effects
        effects = compute_mediation_effects(boot_data, med_model, out_model,
                                          treatment, mediator, outcome, covariates)
        
        push!(boot_nde, effects.nde)
        push!(boot_nie, effects.nie)
        push!(boot_te, effects.te)
    end
    
    return (nde=boot_nde, nie=boot_nie, te=boot_te)
end

5.6.1 8.3.1 Worked Example: Job Training Program

We analyze data from a hypothetical job training program where we wish to understand whether the effect of training (A) on earnings (Y) is mediated by increased job search intensity (M).

Random.seed!(123)
n = 2000

# Generate synthetic mediation data
function generate_mediation_data(n::Int)
    # Baseline covariates
    L₁ = randn(n)                    # Prior work experience  
    L₂ = rand(Bernoulli(0.4), n)     # College education
    
    # Treatment assignment (randomized trial)
    A = rand(Bernoulli(0.5), n)
    
    # Mediator: job search intensity 
    # Affected by treatment and covariates, plus unmeasured factors
    ε_M = randn(n) * 0.8
    M = 2.0 .+ 1.5 * A .+ 0.5 * L₁ .+ 0.8 * L₂ .+ ε_M
    
    # Outcome: log earnings
    # Affected by treatment, mediator, covariates, and their interactions
    ε_Y = randn(n) * 0.6  
    Y = 8.0 .+ 0.6 * A .+ 0.4 * M .+ 0.3 * A .* M .+ 
        0.4 * L₁ .+ 0.5 * L₂ .+ ε_Y
    
    return DataFrame(
        treatment = A,
        mediator = M, 
        outcome = Y,
        experience = L₁,
        college = L₂
    )
end

# Generate and analyze data
data = generate_mediation_data(n)

# Perform mediation analysis
result = mediation_analysis(data, :treatment, :mediator, :outcome, 
                          [:experience, :college])

println(result)

The analysis reveals how the job training program affects earnings through both direct pathways (perhaps increased human capital) and indirect pathways operating through job search behavior.

5.7 8.4 Treatment-Mediator Interactions

When the effect of the mediator on the outcome depends on treatment level, the decomposition of effects becomes more complex. The presence of treatment-mediator interactions affects both the interpretation and identification of mediation effects.

5.7.1 8.4.1 Interaction Effects and Mediation

Consider an outcome model with treatment-mediator interaction: E[Y|A,M,L] = β₀ + β₁A + β₂M + β₃AM + β₄’L

The presence of the interaction term β₃AM implies that the effect of the mediator depends on treatment level, and vice versa. This complicates the interpretation of direct and indirect effects.

Definition 8.4 (Mediation Effects with Interactions). When treatment-mediator interactions are present: - The natural direct effect captures both the “pure” direct effect and the component due to interaction - The natural indirect effect captures the mediated effect including interaction - A three-way decomposition separates the pure direct effect, pure indirect effect, and interaction component

5.7.2 8.4.2 Four-Way Effect Decomposition

VanderWeele and Vansteelandt (2009) propose a four-way decomposition that separates interaction effects:

Definition 8.5 (Four-Way Decomposition). The total effect can be decomposed as: TE = CDE + INTref + INTmed + PIE

Where: - CDE: Controlled direct effect - INTref: Reference interaction (interaction eliminated by setting M to reference level)
- INTmed: Mediated interaction (interaction eliminated by setting A to reference level) - PIE: Pure indirect effect (mediated effect with interaction eliminated)

This decomposition provides a more nuanced understanding of how interactions contribute to the total effect.

function four_way_decomposition(data::DataFrame, 
                               treatment::Symbol,
                               mediator::Symbol, 
                               outcome::Symbol,
                               covariates::Vector{Symbol}=Symbol[];
                               reference_mediator::Real=0.0)
    
    # Fit models with interaction
    med_formula = term(mediator) ~ term(treatment) + sum(term.(covariates))
    out_formula = term(outcome) ~ term(treatment) + term(mediator) + 
                  term(treatment) * term(mediator) + sum(term.(covariates))
    
    med_model = lm(med_formula, data)
    out_model = lm(out_formula, data)
    
    n = nrow(data)
    
    # Create prediction datasets
    data_a1 = copy(data); data_a1[!, treatment] .= 1
    data_a0 = copy(data); data_a0[!, treatment] .= 0
    
    # Predicted mediator values
    m1_pred = predict(med_model, data_a1)  # M(1)
    m0_pred = predict(med_model, data_a0)  # M(0)
    
    # Four-way components
    # Y(1,M(1)) - Y(0,M(0)): Total effect
    data_te_11 = copy(data); data_te_11[!, treatment] .= 1; data_te_11[!, mediator] = m1_pred
    data_te_00 = copy(data); data_te_00[!, treatment] .= 0; data_te_00[!, mediator] = m0_pred
    te = mean(predict(out_model, data_te_11) - predict(out_model, data_te_00))
    
    # Y(1,m*) - Y(0,m*): Controlled direct effect at reference level
    data_cde_1 = copy(data); data_cde_1[!, treatment] .= 1; data_cde_1[!, mediator] .= reference_mediator
    data_cde_0 = copy(data); data_cde_0[!, treatment] .= 0; data_cde_0[!, mediator] .= reference_mediator
    cde = mean(predict(out_model, data_cde_1) - predict(out_model, data_cde_0))
    
    # Y(0,M(1)) - Y(0,M(0)): Pure indirect effect
    data_pie_01 = copy(data); data_pie_01[!, treatment] .= 0; data_pie_01[!, mediator] = m1_pred
    data_pie_00 = copy(data); data_pie_00[!, treatment] .= 0; data_pie_00[!, mediator] = m0_pred
    pie = mean(predict(out_model, data_pie_01) - predict(out_model, data_pie_00))
    
    # Y(1,M(0)) - Y(0,M(0)) - CDE: Reference interaction
    data_intref_10 = copy(data); data_intref_10[!, treatment] .= 1; data_intref_10[!, mediator] = m0_pred
    intref = mean(predict(out_model, data_intref_10) - predict(out_model, data_te_00)) - cde
    
    # Mediated interaction (by residual)
    intmed = te - cde - pie - intref
    
    return (
        total_effect = te,
        controlled_direct = cde, 
        pure_indirect = pie,
        reference_interaction = intref,
        mediated_interaction = intmed,
        proportion_mediated = (pie + intmed) / te
    )
end

5.8 8.5 Multiple Mediators and Path-Specific Effects

In many applications, multiple mediators operate simultaneously, and researchers wish to understand the relative importance of different causal pathways.

5.8.1 8.5.1 Path-Specific Effects in DAGs

Consider a system with treatment A, two mediators M₁ and M₂, and outcome Y, where the causal relationships are represented by a directed acyclic graph (DAG). The total effect of A on Y can be decomposed into path-specific effects corresponding to different routes through the mediators.

Definition 8.6 (Path-Specific Effects). For a DAG with multiple mediators, path-specific effects quantify the effect transmitted through specific combinations of mediating variables while disabling other pathways.

For two mediators, we can define: - Effect through M₁ only: A → M₁ → Y - Effect through M₂ only: A → M₂ → Y
- Effect through both M₁ and M₂: A → M₁ → Y and A → M₂ → Y - Direct effect: A → Y (not through either mediator)

5.8.2 8.5.2 Sequential Mediation

When mediators are causally ordered (e.g., A → M₁ → M₂ → Y), we have sequential mediation. The effect can be decomposed into: - Direct effect: A → Y - Simple indirect effects: A → M₁ → Y and A → M₂ → Y
- Sequential indirect effect: A → M₁ → M₂ → Y

Theorem 8.2 (Identification of Sequential Mediation Effects). Under appropriate ignorability assumptions, sequential mediation effects are identified through iterated expectations over the joint distribution of mediators.

The computational complexity increases rapidly with the number of mediators, often requiring Monte Carlo methods for estimation.

function sequential_mediation_analysis(data::DataFrame,
                                     treatment::Symbol,
                                     mediators::Vector{Symbol}, 
                                     outcome::Symbol,
                                     covariates::Vector{Symbol}=Symbol[];
                                     n_simulations::Int=10000)
    
    # Fit models for each mediator in sequence
    mediator_models = []
    for (i, med) in enumerate(mediators)
        # Include treatment, covariates, and previous mediators
        predictors = [treatment; covariates; mediators[1:i-1]]
        formula = term(med) ~ sum(term.(predictors))
        push!(mediator_models, lm(formula, data))
    end
    
    # Fit outcome model
    all_predictors = [treatment; mediators; covariates]
    out_formula = term(outcome) ~ sum(term.(all_predictors))
    outcome_model = lm(out_formula, data)
    
    # Monte Carlo simulation for path-specific effects
    n = nrow(data)
    
    # Generate potential outcomes under different intervention combinations
    path_effects = Dict{String, Vector{Float64}}()
    
    for sim in 1:n_simulations
        # Sample individual
        i = rand(1:n)
        individual = data[i:i, :]
        
        # Direct effect (set treatment, keep mediators at natural control levels)
        individual_a1 = copy(individual)
        individual_a0 = copy(individual) 
        individual_a1[!, treatment] .= 1
        individual_a0[!, treatment] .= 0
        
        # Simulate mediators under control
        med_values_control = simulate_mediators(individual_a0, mediator_models, mediators)
        
        # Outcome under treatment with mediators at control levels
        individual_direct_1 = copy(individual_a1)
        for (med, val) in zip(mediators, med_values_control)
            individual_direct_1[!, med] = val
        end
        
        individual_direct_0 = copy(individual_a0)
        for (med, val) in zip(mediators, med_values_control)
            individual_direct_0[!, med] = val
        end
        
        y_direct_1 = predict(outcome_model, individual_direct_1)[1]
        y_direct_0 = predict(outcome_model, individual_direct_0)[1]
        
        if !haskey(path_effects, "direct")
            path_effects["direct"] = Float64[]
        end
        push!(path_effects["direct"], y_direct_1 - y_direct_0)
        
        # Total effect  
        med_values_treatment = simulate_mediators(individual_a1, mediator_models, mediators)
        
        individual_total_1 = copy(individual_a1)
        for (med, val) in zip(mediators, med_values_treatment)
            individual_total_1[!, med] = val
        end
        
        y_total_1 = predict(outcome_model, individual_total_1)[1]
        y_total_0 = predict(outcome_model, individual_direct_0)[1]
        
        if !haskey(path_effects, "total")
            path_effects["total"] = Float64[]
        end
        push!(path_effects["total"], y_total_1 - y_total_0)
    end
    
    # Compute summary statistics
    effects_summary = Dict()
    for (path, values) in path_effects
        effects_summary[path] = (
            mean = mean(values),
            std = std(values),
            quantiles = quantile(values, [0.025, 0.975])
        )
    end
    
    return effects_summary
end

function simulate_mediators(data_row, mediator_models, mediators)
    values = Float64[]
    current_data = copy(data_row)
    
    for (i, med) in enumerate(mediators)
        # Predict mediator value
        pred_mean = predict(mediator_models[i], current_data)[1]
        
        # Add noise (assumes homoscedastic residuals)
        residual_std = sqrt(deviance(mediator_models[i]) / dof_residual(mediator_models[i]))
        value = pred_mean + randn() * residual_std
        
        push!(values, value)
        current_data[!, med] .= value  # Update for next mediator
    end
    
    return values
end

5.9 8.6 Sensitivity Analysis for Unmeasured Confounding

The sequential ignorability assumption is strong and often implausible. Sensitivity analysis examines how violations of this assumption affect mediation effect estimates.

5.9.1 8.6.1 Sensitivity Parameters

Following Imai et al. (2010), we parameterize sensitivity to unmeasured confounding using correlations between unmeasured factors and the mediator-outcome relationship.

Definition 8.7 (Sensitivity Parameters). Let U be an unmeasured confounder of the mediator-outcome relationship. Define: - ρ_MU: Correlation between U and M given A, L - ρ_YU: Correlation between U and Y given A, M, L

These parameters quantify the strength of unmeasured confounding required to explain away observed mediation effects.

5.9.2 8.6.2 Sensitivity Analysis Implementation

function sensitivity_analysis_mediation(data::DataFrame,
                                      treatment::Symbol,
                                      mediator::Symbol, 
                                      outcome::Symbol,
                                      covariates::Vector{Symbol}=Symbol[];
                                      rho_grid::AbstractVector=0.0:0.1:0.8)
    
    # Baseline mediation analysis
    baseline = mediation_analysis(data, treatment, mediator, outcome, covariates; n_bootstrap=500)
    
    # Grid search over sensitivity parameters
    sensitivity_results = []
    
    for rho_mu in rho_grid, rho_yu in rho_grid
        # Adjust estimates based on sensitivity parameters
        # This is a simplified version - full implementation would require
        # more sophisticated bias correction formulas
        
        # Bias correction for NIE (simplified)
        bias_correction = rho_mu * rho_yu * std(data[!, mediator]) * std(data[!, outcome])
        
        nie_adjusted = baseline.nie - bias_correction
        nde_adjusted = baseline.nde  # Direct effect less sensitive to M-Y confounding
        te_adjusted = nie_adjusted + nde_adjusted
        
        push!(sensitivity_results, (
            rho_mu = rho_mu,
            rho_yu = rho_yu, 
            nie_adjusted = nie_adjusted,
            nde_adjusted = nde_adjusted,
            te_adjusted = te_adjusted,
            proportion_mediated = nie_adjusted / te_adjusted
        ))
    end
    
    return (baseline = baseline, sensitivity = sensitivity_results)
end

function plot_sensitivity_contours(sensitivity_results)
    # This would create contour plots showing how mediation effects
    # change as a function of sensitivity parameters
    # Implementation would use Makie.jl for visualization
    
    println("Sensitivity Analysis Summary:")
    println("Baseline NIE: $(sensitivity_results.baseline.nie)")
    
    # Find parameter values that would eliminate mediation effect
    critical_points = filter(r -> abs(r.nie_adjusted) < 0.01, sensitivity_results.sensitivity)
    
    if !isempty(critical_points)
        min_rho = minimum([r.rho_mu + r.rho_yu for r in critical_points])
        println("Minimum combined correlation to eliminate NIE: $(round(min_rho, digits=3))")
    else
        println("NIE robust to all examined sensitivity parameter values")
    end
end

5.10 8.7 Interventional Effects and Alternative Estimands

5.10.1 8.7.1 Interventional Direct and Indirect Effects

Natural effects involve cross-world counterfactuals that may be philosophically problematic. Interventional effects provide an alternative that avoids these issues.

Definition 8.8 (Interventional Effects). - Interventional Indirect Effect: E[Y(1,M(1)) - Y(1,M)] where M is set to a fixed distribution - Interventional Direct Effect: E[Y(1,M) - Y(0,M)] where M is set to a fixed distribution

These effects are easier to interpret as policy interventions and avoid some identification challenges of natural effects.

5.10.2 8.7.2 Principal Stratification for Mediation

An alternative approach uses principal stratification, classifying individuals by their potential mediator values under each treatment condition.

Definition 8.9 (Principal Strata for Mediation). Define principal strata based on (M(0), M(1)): - Never-takers: M(0) = M(1) = 0 - Always-takers: M(0) = M(1) = 1
- Compliers: M(0) = 0, M(1) = 1 - Defiers: M(0) = 1, M(1) = 0

This approach focuses on causal effects within principal strata, avoiding cross-world counterfactuals.

5.11 8.8 Worked Example: Smoking Cessation Intervention

We analyze data from a smoking cessation study where a behavioral intervention (A) aims to reduce cigarettes smoked per day (Y), potentially mediated by increased self-efficacy (M).

# Generate smoking cessation data with realistic structure
function generate_smoking_data(n::Int)
    Random.seed!(456)
    
    # Baseline characteristics
    age = 30 .+ 15 * rand(n)                        # Age 30-45
    gender = rand(


\newpage

# Chapter 9: Sensitivity Analysis for Unmeasured Confounding

The identification of causal effects from observational data fundamentally relies on the assumption of no unmeasured confounding—that all variables that affect both treatment assignment and the outcome have been observed and properly adjusted for. This assumption, often called "ignorability" or "conditional exchangeability," is inherently untestable from the data alone. When this assumption is violated, our causal estimates may be severely biased, potentially leading to incorrect scientific conclusions or harmful policy decisions.

This chapter develops formal frameworks for assessing the sensitivity of causal conclusions to violations of the no unmeasured confounding assumption. We examine several complementary approaches: Rosenbaum's sensitivity analysis provides bounds on treatment effects under specified degrees of unmeasured confounding; E-values quantify the minimum strength of association required for an unmeasured confounder to explain away an observed effect; partial identification bounds acknowledge uncertainty while still providing informative ranges for causal effects; and negative control methods offer diagnostic tools to detect unmeasured confounding in practice.

## The Threat of Unmeasured Confounding

Consider the potential outcomes framework introduced in Chapter 1. For individual $i$, let $Y_i(1)$ and $Y_i(0)$ denote the potential outcomes under treatment and control, respectively, with observed outcome $Y_i = A_i Y_i(1) + (1-A_i)Y_i(0)$ where $A_i$ indicates treatment assignment. The average treatment effect (ATE) is defined as:

$$\tau = E[Y_i(1) - Y_i(0)]$$

Given a set of observed covariates $\mathbf{X}_i$, the key identifying assumption is conditional exchangeability:

> **Assumption 9.1 (Conditional Exchangeability).** $(Y_i(1), Y_i(0)) \perp A_i | \mathbf{X}_i$

This states that within levels of $\mathbf{X}_i$, treatment assignment is as good as random with respect to potential outcomes. Under this assumption, along with positivity and consistency, the ATE is identified as:

$$\tau = E[E[Y_i|A_i=1, \mathbf{X}_i] - E[Y_i|A_i=0, \mathbf{X}_i]]$$

However, suppose there exists an unmeasured confounder $U_i$ such that $(Y_i(1), Y_i(0)) \not\perp A_i | \mathbf{X}_i, U_i$ but $(Y_i(1), Y_i(0)) \perp A_i | \mathbf{X}_i, U_i$. Then our estimate based on $\mathbf{X}_i$ alone will generally be biased.

To formalize this threat, consider a simple linear model with unmeasured confounding:

$$Y_i = \alpha + \tau A_i + \boldsymbol{\beta}^T \mathbf{X}_i + \gamma U_i + \epsilon_i$$

$$A_i = \delta_0 + \boldsymbol{\delta}_1^T \mathbf{X}_i + \delta_2 U_i + \eta_i$$

where $U_i$ is unobserved, $\epsilon_i$ and $\eta_i$ are independent error terms, and $\gamma \neq 0$ or $\delta_2 \neq 0$ (or both) so that $U_i$ confounds the relationship. The bias in estimating $\tau$ by ordinary least squares, omitting $U_i$, is approximately:

$$\text{Bias} \approx \frac{\gamma \delta_2}{\text{Var}(A_i|\mathbf{X}_i)}$$

This bias depends on both the strength of association between the unmeasured confounder and the outcome ($\gamma$) and between the confounder and treatment assignment ($\delta_2$). Understanding and quantifying this bias is central to sensitivity analysis.

## Cornfield Conditions

The earliest formal approach to sensitivity analysis traces to Cornfield et al. (1959), who considered the question: how strong must an unmeasured confounder be to explain away an observed association? Their insight was to consider the minimum strength of confounding needed to reduce a significant association to non-significance.

Consider a 2×2 contingency table relating binary treatment $A$ and binary outcome $Y$, with observed odds ratio $\text{OR}_{AY}$. Suppose there exists a binary unmeasured confounder $U$ with the following associations:
- $\text{OR}_{AU} = $ odds ratio between treatment and unmeasured confounder  
- $\text{OR}_{UY} = $ odds ratio between unmeasured confounder and outcome

> **Theorem 9.1 (Cornfield Inequality).** If an unmeasured binary confounder $U$ explains away the entire association between $A$ and $Y$, then:
> $$\text{OR}_{AU} \cdot \text{OR}_{UY} \geq \text{OR}_{AY}$$

*Proof.* Under the assumption that $U$ fully accounts for the association between $A$ and $Y$, we have conditional independence: $A \perp Y | U$. This implies $\text{OR}_{AY|U=0} = \text{OR}_{AY|U=1} = 1$.

The crude odds ratio can be expressed as a weighted average of the stratum-specific odds ratios:
$$\text{OR}_{AY} = \frac{\text{OR}_{AY|U=1} \cdot w_1 + \text{OR}_{AY|U=0} \cdot w_0}{w_1 + w_0}$$

where the weights depend on the prevalence of $U$ and the distribution of $A$. Through algebraic manipulation of the cell probabilities and the constraint that stratum-specific odds ratios equal 1, one can show that:
$$\text{OR}_{AY} \leq \text{OR}_{AU} \cdot \text{OR}_{UY}$$

with equality achieved under certain boundary conditions. □

This result suggests that for an unmeasured confounder to fully explain away an observed odds ratio of magnitude $\text{OR}_{AY}$, the product of its associations with treatment and outcome must be at least as large. If we observe $\text{OR}_{AY} = 4$, then an unmeasured confounder would need $\text{OR}_{AU} \cdot \text{OR}_{UY} \geq 4$ to explain this association away entirely.

The Cornfield condition provides a useful threshold for evaluating plausibility. If domain knowledge suggests that no unmeasured confounder could reasonably have associations as strong as required by the inequality, this lends credibility to a causal interpretation of the observed association.

## Rosenbaum Sensitivity Analysis

Rosenbaum (1987, 2002) developed a comprehensive framework for sensitivity analysis in matched observational studies that has become the gold standard for assessing robustness to unmeasured confounding. The approach models the treatment assignment mechanism in the presence of an unmeasured confounder and derives bounds on the significance level and magnitude of treatment effects.

### The Rosenbaum Model

Consider $N$ matched pairs $(i,j)$ where unit $i$ received treatment ($A_i = 1$) and unit $j$ received control ($A_j = 0$). Let $\mathbf{x}_i$ and $\mathbf{x}_j$ denote observed covariates, assumed identical within pairs for exact matching: $\mathbf{x}_i = \mathbf{x}_j$. Let $u_i$ and $u_j$ denote unobserved scalar confounders.

The Rosenbaum model specifies the odds of treatment assignment as:
$$\frac{\pi_k}{1-\pi_k} = \exp(\boldsymbol{\beta}^T \mathbf{x}_k + \gamma u_k)$$

where $\pi_k = P(A_k = 1 | \mathbf{x}_k, u_k)$ and $\gamma$ measures the strength of the unmeasured confounder's effect on treatment assignment.

Within matched pairs with identical observed covariates, the odds ratio of treatment assignment becomes:
$$\frac{\pi_i(1-\pi_j)}{\pi_j(1-\pi_i)} = \exp[\gamma(u_i - u_j)]$$

> **Definition 9.1 (Sensitivity Parameter).** The sensitivity parameter $\Gamma \geq 1$ bounds the odds ratio of treatment assignment within matched pairs:
> $$\frac{1}{\Gamma} \leq \frac{\pi_i(1-\pi_j)}{\pi_j(1-\pi_i)} \leq \Gamma$$

When $\Gamma = 1$, there is no unmeasured confounding and treatment assignment within pairs is determined solely by random variation. As $\Gamma$ increases, the degree of potential unmeasured confounding grows stronger.

### Bounds on Treatment Effects

Let $R_k$ denote the signed rank of the absolute treatment effect within matched pairs, and let $T^+ = \sum_{k: Y_{1k} > Y_{0k}} R_k$ denote the Wilcoxon signed rank statistic. Under the null hypothesis of no treatment effect, $T^+$ has a known distribution when $\Gamma = 1$.

With unmeasured confounding parameterized by $\Gamma$, the distribution of $T^+$ is no longer known exactly, but Rosenbaum derives bounds.

> **Theorem 9.2 (Rosenbaum Bounds).** Under the Rosenbaum model with sensitivity parameter $\Gamma$, the significance level $p$ for testing no treatment effect satisfies:
> $$p^-(\Gamma) \leq p \leq p^+(\Gamma)$$
> where $p^-(\Gamma)$ and $p^+(\Gamma)$ are calculable bounds that depend on $\Gamma$ and the observed data.

The bounds $p^-(\Gamma)$ and $p^+(\Gamma)$ represent the most favorable and least favorable scenarios for the treatment effect under the specified degree of unmeasured confounding. As $\Gamma$ increases, the bounds generally widen, reflecting greater uncertainty about the true treatment effect.

For point estimates, Rosenbaum derives analogous bounds. Let $\hat{\tau}(\Gamma)$ denote a point estimate of the treatment effect under sensitivity parameter $\Gamma$:

$$\hat{\tau}^-(\Gamma) \leq \hat{\tau} \leq \hat{\tau}^+(\Gamma)$$

These bounds show how the estimated treatment effect might change under different assumptions about unmeasured confounding strength.

### Computational Implementation

The following Julia code implements Rosenbaum sensitivity analysis for matched pairs data:

```julia
using Distributions, StatsBase, LinearAlgebra

struct RosenbaumResult
    gamma_values::Vector{Float64}
    p_lower::Vector{Float64}
    p_upper::Vector{Float64}
    tau_lower::Vector{Float64}
    tau_upper::Vector{Float64}
end

function rosenbaum_sensitivity(treated_outcomes::Vector{Float64}, 
                             control_outcomes::Vector{Float64},
                             gamma_range::Vector{Float64})
    
    n_pairs = length(treated_outcomes)
    @assert n_pairs == length(control_outcomes) "Unequal number of pairs"
    
    # Calculate paired differences and their signs
    differences = treated_outcomes .- control_outcomes
    abs_differences = abs.(differences)
    signs = sign.(differences)
    
    # Remove pairs with zero difference
    nonzero_idx = abs_differences .> 1e-10
    abs_diff_nz = abs_differences[nonzero_idx]
    signs_nz = signs[nonzero_idx]
    n_nz = length(abs_diff_nz)
    
    # Compute ranks of absolute differences
    ranks = ordinalrank(abs_diff_nz)
    
    # Wilcoxon signed rank statistic
    W_plus = sum(ranks[signs_nz .> 0])
    
    # Initialize result vectors
    p_lower = Float64[]
    p_upper = Float64[]
    tau_lower = Float64[]
    tau_upper = Float64[]
    
    for gamma in gamma_range
        # Expected value and variance under different scenarios
        if gamma == 1.0
            # No confounding case
            exp_W = n_nz * (n_nz + 1) / 4
            var_W = n_nz * (n_nz + 1) * (2 * n_nz + 1) / 24
        else
            # With confounding - compute bounds
            # Most favorable scenario (lower bound on p-value)
            p_plus = gamma / (1 + gamma)  # Probability favoring treatment
            exp_W_plus = sum(ranks) * p_plus
            var_W_plus = sum(ranks.^2) * p_plus * (1 - p_plus)
            
            # Least favorable scenario (upper bound on p-value)  
            p_minus = 1 / (1 + gamma)  # Probability against treatment
            exp_W_minus = sum(ranks) * p_minus
            var_W_minus = sum(ranks.^2) * p_minus * (1 - p_minus)
            
            # Normal approximation for bounds
            z_plus = (W_plus - 0.5 - exp_W_minus) / sqrt(var_W_minus)
            z_minus = (W_plus + 0.5 - exp_W_plus) / sqrt(var_W_plus)
            
            p_lower_val = 1 - cdf(Normal(), z_minus)  # Lower bound on p-value
            p_upper_val = 1 - cdf(Normal(), z_plus)   # Upper bound on p-value
            
            push!(p_lower, max(0.0, p_lower_val))
            push!(p_upper, min(1.0, p_upper_val))
        end
        
        # Point estimates bounds (simplified Hodges-Lehmann estimator)
        median_diff = median(differences)
        # In practice, would compute exact Hodges-Lehmann bounds
        # Here we provide approximation
        se_approx = std(differences) / sqrt(n_pairs)
        adjustment = log(gamma) * se_approx
        
        push!(tau_lower, median_diff - adjustment)
        push!(tau_upper, median_diff + adjustment)
    end
    
    return RosenbaumResult(gamma_range, p_lower, p_upper, tau_lower, tau_upper)
end

# Example usage with simulated matched pairs data
function simulate_matched_pairs(n_pairs::Int, true_effect::Float64, 
                               confounding_strength::Float64)
    # Simulate unmeasured confounder
    U = randn(2 * n_pairs)
    
    # Treatment assignment with confounding
    logit_prob = confounding_strength * U
    prob_treatment = 1 ./ (1 .+ exp.(-logit_prob))
    treatment_assignment = rand(2 * n_pairs) .< prob_treatment
    
    # Create matched pairs (simplified matching)
    treated_idx = findall(treatment_assignment)[1:n_pairs]
    control_idx = findall(.!treatment_assignment)[1:n_pairs]
    
    # Simulate outcomes with confounding
    Y_treated = true_effect .+ 0.5 * U[treated_idx] + randn(n_pairs)
    Y_control = 0.5 * U[control_idx] + randn(n_pairs)
    
    return Y_treated, Y_control
end

# Run sensitivity analysis
n_pairs = 100
Y_treated, Y_control = simulate_matched_pairs(n_pairs, 0.5, 0.3)
gamma_values = [1.0, 1.2, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0]
results = rosenbaum_sensitivity(Y_treated, Y_control, gamma_values)

println("Rosenbaum Sensitivity Analysis Results:")
println("Γ\tP-value Lower\tP-value Upper\tEffect Lower\tEffect Upper")
for i in 1:length(gamma_values)
    @printf("%.1f\t%.4f\t\t%.4f\t\t%.3f\t\t%.3f\n", 
            results.gamma_values[i], results.p_lower[i], results.p_upper[i],
            results.tau_lower[i], results.tau_upper[i])
end

This implementation shows how statistical significance and effect size estimates change as we allow for stronger unmeasured confounding (larger \(\Gamma\) values). As \(\Gamma\) increases, the bounds on p-values typically widen, and the range of plausible effect sizes expands.

5.12 E-values: Minimum Strength of Confounding

While Rosenbaum sensitivity analysis provides bounds under specified levels of confounding, researchers often want to know: what is the minimum strength of unmeasured confounding needed to explain away an observed effect? VanderWeele and Ding (2017) introduced the E-value to answer this question precisely.

Definition 9.2 (E-value). The E-value is the minimum strength of association on the risk ratio scale that an unmeasured confounder must have with both the treatment and outcome to fully explain away a specific treatment-outcome association, conditional on the measured covariates.

For a true risk ratio \(RR_{true}\), an observed risk ratio \(RR_{obs}\), and unmeasured confounder associations \(RR_{UD}\) (confounder-outcome) and \(RR_{UA}\) (confounder-treatment), the relationship is:

\[RR_{obs} = RR_{true} \cdot \frac{RR_{UD} \cdot p_1 + (1-p_1)}{RR_{UD} \cdot p_0 + (1-p_0)}\]

where \(p_1\) and \(p_0\) are the prevalences of the unmeasured confounder in treated and control groups.

Theorem 9.3 (E-value Formula). For an observed risk ratio \(RR_{obs}\), the E-value is: \[E\text{-value} = RR_{obs} + \sqrt{RR_{obs}(RR_{obs} - 1)}\] when \(RR_{obs} \geq 1\), and: \[E\text{-value} = \frac{1}{RR_{obs}} + \sqrt{\frac{1}{RR_{obs}}\left(\frac{1}{RR_{obs}} - 1\right)}\] when \(RR_{obs} < 1\).

Proof. To derive the E-value, we find the minimum value of \(RR_{UD} = RR_{UA} = E\) such that the observed association could be entirely due to confounding (i.e., \(RR_{true} = 1\)).

Setting \(RR_{true} = 1\) and \(RR_{UD} = RR_{UA} = E\) in the bias formula, and assuming the worst-case scenario for confounding prevalence, we get:

\[RR_{obs} = \frac{E \cdot p_1 + (1-p_1)}{E \cdot p_0 + (1-p_0)}\]

The maximum bias occurs when the unmeasured confounder is most prevalent in one group and least prevalent in the other. Through calculus of variations, this leads to the boundary conditions that yield the E-value formula. □

The E-value provides an intuitive benchmark: if no unmeasured confounder could plausibly have associations with both treatment and outcome as strong as the E-value, then unmeasured confounding is unlikely to explain away the observed effect.

5.12.1 E-values for Different Effect Measures

The E-value concept extends beyond risk ratios to other effect measures:

Odds Ratio: For an odds ratio \(OR\): \[E\text{-value} = OR + \sqrt{OR(OR-1)}\]

Standardized Mean Difference: For a standardized mean difference \(d\) (Cohen’s d): \[E\text{-value} = \exp(0.91 \cdot d)\] This approximation works well for moderate effect sizes.

Hazard Ratio: For survival analysis with hazard ratio \(HR\): \[E\text{-value} = HR + \sqrt{HR(HR-1)}\]

5.12.2 Computational Implementation of E-values

using Distributions

"""
Calculate E-value for different effect measures
"""
struct EValueResult
    point_estimate_evalue::Float64
    ci_lower_evalue::Float64
    interpretation::String
end

function calculate_evalue(effect_measure::String, estimate::Float64, 
                         ci_lower::Float64, ci_upper::Float64)
    
    function evalue_rr(rr::Float64)
        if rr >= 1
            return rr + sqrt(rr * (rr - 1))
        else
            rr_inv = 1/rr
            return rr_inv + sqrt(rr_inv * (rr_inv - 1))
        end
    end
    
    function evalue_or(or::Float64)
        return evalue_rr(or)  # Same formula as RR
    end
    
    function evalue_cohen_d(d::Float64)
        return exp(0.91 * abs(d))
    end
    
    # Calculate E-value for point estimate
    if effect_measure == "RR" || effect_measure == "HR"
        point_evalue = evalue_rr(estimate)
        ci_evalue = evalue_rr(abs(estimate) > abs(1/estimate) ? ci_lower : ci_upper)
    elseif effect_measure == "OR"
        point_evalue = evalue_or(estimate)
        ci_evalue = evalue_or(abs(estimate) > abs(1/estimate) ? ci_lower : ci_upper)
    elseif effect_measure == "Cohen_d"
        point_evalue = evalue_cohen_d(estimate)
        # For confidence interval, use the bound closer to null
        ci_bound = abs(ci_lower) > abs(ci_upper) ? ci_upper : ci_lower
        ci_evalue = evalue_cohen_d(ci_bound)
    else
        error("Unsupported effect measure: $effect_measure")
    end
    
    # Interpretation
    interpretation = """
    E-value for point estimate: $(round(point_evalue, digits=2))
    E-value for confidence interval: $(round(ci_evalue, digits=2))
    
    Interpretation: An unmeasured confounder would need to be associated with both
    the treatment and outcome by a risk ratio of $(round(point_evalue, digits=2))-fold
    each to fully explain away the point estimate, or $(round(ci_evalue, digits=2))-fold
    each to explain away the confidence interval.
    """
    
    return EValueResult(point_evalue, ci_evalue, interpretation)
end

# Example: E-value calculation for a clinical trial
# Suppose we observe RR = 2.5 with 95% CI: [1.8, 3.5]
rr_estimate = 2.5
rr_ci_lower = 1.8
rr_ci_upper = 3.5

evalue_result = calculate_evalue("RR", rr_estimate, rr_ci_lower, rr_ci_upper)
println(evalue_result.interpretation)

# Example: E-value for Cohen's d = 0.6 with CI [0.3, 0.9]
d_estimate = 0.6
d_ci_lower = 0.3
d_ci_upper = 0.9

evalue_d_result = calculate_evalue("Cohen_d", d_estimate, d_ci_lower, d_ci_upper)
println(evalue_d_result.interpretation)

5.12.3 Interpreting E-values

E-values should be interpreted in the context of domain knowledge about plausible confounders. Consider these guidelines:

  1. E-value < 1.5: The observed effect could be explained away by relatively weak confounding. Exercise caution in causal interpretation.

  2. E-value 1.5-2.0: Moderate strength confounding would be required. Consider whether known risk factors could plausibly have such associations.

  3. E-value > 2.0: Strong confounding would be needed. Less likely that unmeasured factors could explain away the effect, especially if known strong risk factors have been controlled for.

  4. E-value > 3.0: Very strong confounding required. Provides substantial robustness to unmeasured confounding concerns.

The key insight is that E-values quantify the robustness of causal claims to unmeasured confounding in terms that researchers can evaluate against their domain expertise.

5.13 Ding-VanderWeele Sensitivity Analysis

Ding and VanderWeele (2016) developed a parametric approach to sensitivity analysis that directly models the unmeasured confounder and provides identification results under weaker assumptions than traditional methods.

5.13.1 The Framework

Consider the outcome model: \[E[Y|A, \mathbf{X}, U] = \theta_0 + \theta_1 A + \boldsymbol{\theta}_2^T \mathbf{X} + \theta_3 U\]

and treatment model: \[E[A|\mathbf{X}, U] = \alpha_0 + \boldsymbol{\alpha}_1^T \mathbf{X} + \alpha_2 U\]

where \(U\) is an unmeasured binary confounder with \(P(U=1) = p_U\).

Theorem 9.4 (Ding-VanderWeele Identification). Under the above models, the average treatment effect is identified as: \[\tau = \theta_1 + \frac{\theta_3 \alpha_2}{1 + \exp(\alpha_0 + \boldsymbol{\alpha}_1^T \overline{\mathbf{X}})} \cdot \frac{p_U}{1-p_U}\] where \(\overline{\mathbf{X}}\) is the sample mean of covariates, provided we can bound or specify the confounding parameters \(\theta_3\), \(\alpha_2\), and \(p_U\).

The approach requires researchers to specify plausible ranges for the confounding parameters based on domain knowledge or external data. This parameterization allows for more flexible sensitivity analysis than the Rosenbaum approach, particularly in non-matched settings.

5.13.2 Sensitivity Parameters

The method introduces three key sensitivity parameters:

  1. \(\theta_3\): Association between unmeasured confounder and outcome
  2. \(\alpha_2\): Association between unmeasured confounder and treatment
  3. \(p_U\): Prevalence of unmeasured confounder

Researchers specify plausible ranges for these parameters, and the method computes the range of treatment effects consistent with these specifications.

using Optim

"""
Ding-VanderWeele sensitivity analysis for unmeasured binary confounder
"""
function ding_vanderweele_sensitivity(Y::Vector{Float64}, A::Vector{Int}, 
                                    X::Matrix{Float64},
                                    theta3_range::Tuple{Float64, Float64},
                                    alpha2_range::Tuple{Float64, Float64}, 
                                    pU_range::Tuple{Float64, Float64})
    
    n, p = size(X)
    X_mean = mean(X, dims=1)[1, :]
    
    # Fit observed data models (ignoring U)
    # Outcome model: Y ~ A + X
    X_aug = hcat(ones(n), A, X)  # Add intercept and treatment
    beta_hat = (X_aug' * X_aug) \ (X_aug' * Y)
    theta1_obs = beta_hat[2]  # Observed treatment effect
    
    # Treatment model: A ~ X (logistic regression approximation)
    # For simplicity, using linear probability model
    X_treat = hcat(ones(n), X)
    alpha_hat = (X_treat' * X_treat) \ (X_treat' * A)
    
    # Function to compute adjusted treatment effect
    function adjusted_effect(theta3::Float64, alpha2::Float64, pU::Float64)
        # Logistic term for treatment model
        logistic_term = 1 / (1 + exp(alpha_hat[1] + dot(alpha_hat[2:end], X_mean)))
        
        # Adjustment for unmeasured confounding
        adjustment = (theta3 * alpha2 * logistic_term * pU) / (1 - pU)
        
        return theta1_obs + adjustment
    end
    
    # Compute bounds over sensitivity parameter ranges
    tau_min = Inf
    tau_max = -Inf
    
    # Grid search over parameter space
    theta3_grid = range(theta3_range[1], theta3_range[2], length=20)
    alpha2_grid = range(alpha2_range[1], alpha2_range[2], length=20)
    pU_grid = range(max(0.01, pU_range[1]), min(0.99, pU_range[2]), length=20)
    
    for theta3 in theta3_grid, alpha2 in alpha2_grid, pU in pU_grid
        tau_adj = adjusted_effect(theta3, alpha2, pU)
        tau_min = min(tau_min, tau_adj)
        tau_max = max(tau_max, tau_adj)
    end
    
    return (observed_effect = theta1_obs, 
            adjusted_range = (tau_min, tau_max),
            sensitivity_params = (theta3_range, alpha2_range, pU_range))
end

# Example application with simulated data
n = 500
p = 3
X = randn(n, p)
U_true = rand(n) .< 0.3  # 30% prevalence of unmeasured confounder

# Treatment assignment with confounding
A_prob = 1 ./ (1 .+ exp.(-(0.2 .+ 0.1 * sum(X, dims=2)[:] + 0.5 * U_true)))
A = Int.(rand(n) .< A_prob)

# Outcome with confounding
Y = 0.5 * A + 0.2 * sum(X, dims=2)[:] + 0.3 * U_true + randn(n)

# Sensitivity analysis
# Suppose we believe theta3 ∈ [-0.5, 0.5], alpha2 ∈ [-0.8, 0.8], pU ∈ [0.1, 0.5]
results = ding_vanderweele_sensitivity(Y, A, X, 
                                     (-0.5, 0.5),    # theta3 range
                                     (-0.8, 0.8),    # alpha2 range  
                                     (0.1, 0.5))     # pU range

println("Ding-VanderWeele Sensitivity Analysis:")
println("Observed effect (ignoring U): $(round(results.observed_effect, digits=3))")
println("Adjusted effect range: [$(round(results.adjusted_range[1], digits=3)), $(round(results.adjusted_range[2], digits=3))]")
println("True effect: 0.5")

This approach is particularly valuable when researchers have substantive knowledge about the likely strength and prevalence of unmeasured confounders, as it translates that knowledge directly into bounds on causal effects.

5.14 Partial Identification and Manski Bounds

Rather than assuming specific functional forms for confounding, Manski (1990, 2003) developed a partial identification approach that acknowledges fundamental limits to what can be learned from data alone while still providing informative bounds.

5.14.1 The Partial Identification Framework

Consider the average treatment effect \(\tau = E[Y(1)] - E[Y(0)]\). Even without the conditional exchangeability assumption, the data impose logical constraints on the potential outcomes.

Let \(P(Y=1|A=1) = p_{11}\) and \(P(Y=1|A=0) = p_{01}\) denote the observed conditional probabilities for a binary outcome. The potential outcome means are bounded by:

\[p_{11} \leq E[Y(1)] \leq p_{11} + (1-\pi)\] \[p_{01} - (1-\pi) \leq E[Y(0)] \leq p_{01}\]

where \(\pi = P(A=1)\) is the treatment probability.

Theorem 9.5 (Manski Bounds). Without additional assumptions, the average treatment effect is bounded by: $$[p_{11} - p_{01} - (1-

6 Chapter 10: Causal Inference with Time-Varying Treatments

6.1 Introduction

In many longitudinal studies, treatments vary over time, and the presence of time-varying confounding creates fundamental challenges for causal inference. Unlike the static treatment settings explored in previous chapters, time-varying treatments introduce complex feedback mechanisms where past treatments affect future confounders, which in turn affect future treatment decisions. This creates what we term “treatment-confounder feedback,” a phenomenon that renders standard regression methods inappropriate and potentially misleading.

Consider a clinical study examining the effect of antiretroviral therapy (ART) initiation timing on HIV progression. CD4 count serves as both a time-varying confounder (it affects the decision to initiate treatment) and an intermediate variable affected by prior treatment. Standard methods that condition on CD4 count at each time point inadvertently condition on a collider, inducing spurious associations and biasing causal effect estimates.

This chapter develops the theoretical framework and computational methods for addressing time-varying confounding. We establish why traditional approaches fail, then present three major methodological approaches: marginal structural models (MSMs) with inverse probability weighting, structural nested models with g-estimation, and the parametric g-formula. We conclude with modern applications to dynamic treatment regimes and target trial emulation.

6.2 The Problem of Time-Varying Confounding

6.2.1 Notation and Setup

Consider a longitudinal study with discrete time points t = 0, 1, …, K. Let A_t denote treatment status at time t, L_t the vector of time-varying covariates measured at time t, and Y the final outcome measured at time K+1. We use overbars to denote treatment and covariate histories: Ā_t = (A_0, A_1, …, A_t) and L̄_t = (L_0, L_1, …, L_t).

The fundamental challenge arises when the causal graph contains the pattern L_t → A_t → L_{t+1}, indicating that time-varying confounders L_t affect treatment A_t, which subsequently affects future confounders L_{t+1}. This creates treatment-confounder feedback that violates the assumptions underlying standard regression approaches.

6.2.2 Why Standard Methods Fail

To understand the failure of standard regression, consider the simple case with two time points. Suppose we wish to estimate the effect of treatment sequence ā = (a_0, a_1) on outcome Y. A naive approach might fit the regression model:

E[Y | Ā_1 = ā, L̄_1 = l̄] = β_0 + β_1 a_0 + β_2 a_1 + γ_0’ l_0 + γ_1’ l_1

However, this conditions on the post-treatment variable L_1, which may be a collider if affected by prior treatment A_0. Conditioning on a collider opens non-causal paths and induces bias.

Theorem 10.1 (Bias of Standard Regression). Consider a longitudinal setting with treatment-confounder feedback where L_1 is affected by A_0. Then the regression coefficient β_1 from the model E[Y | Ā_1, L̄_1] does not generally identify the causal effect of A_1 on Y.

Proof. Under treatment-confounder feedback, L_1 lies on the causal path A_0 → L_1. When we condition on L_1, we condition on a mediator for the A_0 → Y effect and a collider on paths A_0 ← U → Y → L_1, where U represents unmeasured confounding. This conditioning induces non-causal associations between A_1 and Y through the opened collider paths. □

6.2.3 The Sequential Exchangeability Assumption

To proceed with causal inference in the time-varying setting, we require a temporal extension of the exchangeability assumption.

Definition 10.1 (Sequential Exchangeability). The sequential exchangeability assumption holds if:

Y(ā) ⊥ A_t | L̄t, Ā{t-1} for all t = 0, 1, …, K

where Y(ā) denotes the potential outcome under treatment sequence ā.

This assumption states that at each time t, treatment assignment is conditionally independent of all potential outcomes, given observed covariate and treatment history. Sequential exchangeability is weaker than requiring no unmeasured confounding at any single time point, but stronger than cross-sectional exchangeability.

6.3 Marginal Structural Models

6.3.1 Theoretical Foundation

Marginal structural models (MSMs) parameterize the marginal distribution of potential outcomes across all possible treatment sequences. Unlike conditional models that specify E[Y | A, L], MSMs model E[Y(ā)] directly.

Definition 10.2 (Marginal Structural Model). A marginal structural model is a model for the mean potential outcome:

E[Y(ā)] = g(ā; β)

where g is a known link function and β is the parameter vector of interest.

For binary treatments, a common MSM specification is:

E[Y(ā)] = β_0 + β_1 ∑{t=0}^K a_t + β_2 ∑{t=0}^K t · a_t + β_3 (∑_{t=0}^K a_t)^2

This model includes terms for cumulative exposure, recency effects, and nonlinear dose-response relationships.

6.3.2 Inverse Probability of Treatment Weighting

The key insight of MSMs is that we can consistently estimate β by creating a pseudo-population where treatment assignment is independent of confounders at all time points. This is achieved through inverse probability of treatment weighting (IPTW).

Definition 10.3 (Stabilized IPTW Weights). The stabilized weight for individual i at time t is:

SW_i(t) = ∏_{k=0}^t

where the numerator contains only treatment history and the denominator includes the full covariate history.

These weights have the property that E[SW_i(K)] = 1 under correct model specification, providing built-in diagnostics for model adequacy.

Theorem 10.2 (Consistency of MSM Estimator). Under sequential exchangeability, positivity, and correct model specification, the weighted least squares estimator:

β̂ = argmin_β ∑_i SW_i(K) (Y_i - g(Ā_i; β))^2

is consistent for the parameters of the marginal structural model.

Proof. The IPTW creates a pseudo-population where treatment assignment is independent of time-varying confounders. In this pseudo-population, the observed data distribution equals the distribution that would arise under randomized treatment assignment. Standard least squares theory then applies. □

6.3.3 Computational Implementation

using DataFrames, GLM, StatsModels, Distributions, Random

function compute_iptw_weights(data::DataFrame, treatment_vars::Vector{String}, 
                            covariate_vars::Vector{String})
    """
    Compute stabilized IPTW weights for longitudinal data
    """
    n = nrow(data)
    weights = ones(n)
    
    for t in 1:length(treatment_vars)
        # Numerator: P(A_t | A_{t-1}, ..., A_0)  
        if t == 1
            num_formula = @formula(A ~ 1)
        else
            num_vars = treatment_vars[1:(t-1)]
            num_formula = Term(:A) ~ sum(Term.(Symbol.(num_vars)))
        end
        
        # Denominator: P(A_t | L_t, A_{t-1}, ..., A_0, L_{t-1}, ..., L_0)
        if t == 1
            denom_vars = covariate_vars[1:1]  # Only baseline covariates
        else
            denom_vars = vcat(treatment_vars[1:(t-1)], 
                            covariate_vars[1:t])
        end
        denom_formula = Term(:A) ~ sum(Term.(Symbol.(denom_vars)))
        
        # Fit logistic regression models
        current_data = copy(data)
        current_data.A = data[:, treatment_vars[t]]
        
        num_model = glm(num_formula, current_data, Binomial(), LogitLink())
        denom_model = glm(denom_formula, current_data, Binomial(), LogitLink())
        
        # Compute probability ratios
        num_probs = predict(num_model)
        denom_probs = predict(denom_model)
        
        # Update weights
        treatment_received = data[:, treatment_vars[t]]
        prob_ratios = (treatment_received .* num_probs .+ 
                      (1 .- treatment_received) .* (1 .- num_probs)) ./
                     (treatment_received .* denom_probs .+ 
                      (1 .- treatment_received) .* (1 .- denom_probs))
        
        weights .*= prob_ratios
    end
    
    return weights
end

function fit_msm(data::DataFrame, weights::Vector{Float64}, 
                outcome_var::String, treatment_vars::Vector{String})
    """
    Fit marginal structural model using IPTW
    """
    # Create cumulative treatment exposure
    data.cum_treatment = sum(data[:, treatment_vars], dims=2)[:, 1]
    
    # Create treatment-time interactions
    data.treatment_time = sum(t * data[:, treatment_vars[t]] 
                             for t in 1:length(treatment_vars))
    
    # Fit weighted regression
    formula = @formula(Y ~ cum_treatment + treatment_time + cum_treatment^2)
    model_data = copy(data)
    model_data.Y = data[:, outcome_var]
    
    # Use weights in GLM
    return glm(formula, model_data, Normal(), IdentityLink(), 
              wts=weights)
end

# Example usage with simulated data
Random.seed!(123)
n = 1000
K = 3  # Number of time points

# Simulate longitudinal data with treatment-confounder feedback
data = DataFrame()
data.id = 1:n
data.L0 = rand(Normal(0, 1), n)  # Baseline confounder
data.A0 = rand.(Bernoulli.(logistic.(0.5 .+ 0.3 .* data.L0)))

for t in 1:K
    # Time-varying confounder affected by prior treatment
    prev_A = t == 1 ? data.A0 : data[:, Symbol("A$(t-1)")]
    prev_L = data[:, Symbol("L$(t-1)")]
    data[:, Symbol("L$t")] = prev_L .+ 0.4 .* prev_A .+ rand(Normal(0, 0.5), n)
    
    # Treatment affected by current confounder and treatment history
    if t < K
        treatment_logit = -0.5 .+ 0.3 .* data[:, Symbol("L$t")] .+ 
                         0.2 .* prev_A
        data[:, Symbol("A$t")] = rand.(Bernoulli.(logistic.(treatment_logit)))
    end
end

# Outcome affected by treatment history and time-varying confounders
treatment_history = data.A0 .+ data.A1 .+ data.A2
final_L = data.L3
data.Y = 2.0 .+ 0.5 .* treatment_history .+ 0.3 .* final_L .+ 
         rand(Normal(0, 1), n)

# Compute IPTW weights
treatment_vars = ["A0", "A1", "A2"]
covariate_vars = ["L0", "L1", "L2", "L3"]
weights = compute_iptw_weights(data, treatment_vars, covariate_vars)

# Fit MSM
msm_model = fit_msm(data, weights, "Y", treatment_vars)
println("MSM Results:")
println(msm_model)

6.4 Structural Nested Models

6.4.1 G-Estimation Framework

While MSMs model marginal effects averaged over the study population, structural nested models focus on individual-level causal effects. These models specify how treatment at each time point affects the outcome, conditional on treatment and covariate history.

Definition 10.4 (Structural Nested Mean Model). A structural nested mean model (SNMM) specifies:

E[Y(ā_{t+1:K}, 0, ā_{0:t-1}) | L̄t, Ā{t-1}] = E[Y(ā) | L̄t, Ā{t-1}] - γ(L̄t, Ā{t-1}; ψ) · a_t

where γ(L̄t, Ā{t-1}; ψ) represents the blip function describing the individual-level treatment effect.

The blip function γ captures how the effect of treatment at time t varies with covariate and treatment history. A common specification is:

γ(L̄t, Ā{t-1}; ψ) = ψ_0 + ψ_1’ L_t + ψ_2 ∑_{k=0}^{t-1} A_k

6.4.2 G-Estimation Algorithm

G-estimation proceeds recursively, working backwards from the final time point. The algorithm exploits the fact that under correct model specification, the residual after removing the estimated treatment effect should be independent of treatment.

Algorithm 10.1 (G-Estimation). 1. For t = K, K-1, …, 0: 2. Compute H_t(ψ) = Y - ∑{k=t+1}^K γ(L̄{k-1}, Ā_{k-2}; ψ) · A_k 3. Find ψ_t such that E[H_t(ψ_t) | L̄t, Ā{t-1}, A_t = 1] = E[H_t(ψ_t) | L̄t, Ā{t-1}, A_t = 0] 4. Update ψ = (ψ_0, …, ψ_t)

The estimating equation in step 3 can be implemented using instrumental variable methods or by solving the orthogonality condition directly.

using Optim, LinearAlgebra

function g_estimation(data::DataFrame, treatment_vars::Vector{String},
                     covariate_vars::Vector{String}, outcome_var::String)
    """
    Implement G-estimation for structural nested models
    """
    n = nrow(data)
    K = length(treatment_vars)
    
    function blip_function(L_bar, A_bar, psi)
        # Simple blip function: psi[1] + psi[2] * current_L + psi[3] * cum_A
        if isempty(A_bar)
            cum_A = 0.0
        else
            cum_A = sum(A_bar)
        end
        current_L = L_bar[end]
        return psi[1] + psi[2] * current_L + psi[3] * cum_A
    end
    
    function estimating_equation(psi, t)
        # Compute H_t(psi) for all individuals
        H = copy(data[:, outcome_var])
        
        # Subtract estimated effects of future treatments
        for k in (t+1):K
            for i in 1:n
                if k <= length(treatment_vars)
                    A_history = [data[i, treatment_vars[j]] for j in 1:(k-1)]
                    L_history = [data[i, covariate_vars[j]] for j in 1:k]
                    
                    blip_val = blip_function(L_history, A_history, psi)
                    H[i] -= blip_val * data[i, treatment_vars[k]]
                end
            end
        end
        
        # Compute orthogonality condition
        if t >= 1 && t <= length(treatment_vars)
            A_t = data[:, treatment_vars[t]]
            # Use treatment as instrument for itself
            orthogonality = sum(A_t .* H) / sum(A_t) - 
                          sum((1 .- A_t) .* H) / sum(1 .- A_t)
        else
            orthogonality = 0.0
        end
        
        return orthogonality^2
    end
    
    # Initialize parameters
    psi_hat = zeros(3)
    
    # G-estimation working backwards
